JOHOR BARU: Johor Corp (JCorp), the principal development institution and a leading economic enabler for Johor, has established its maiden sustainable finance framework, the first of its kind by a Malaysian development institution with a diversified, multi-sector portfolio. Maybank Investment Bank Bhd (Maybank IB) has been appointed as the sole sustainability structuring adviser for the framework.

The pilot will explore on-chain cross-border payments involving ringgit and other Asean deposit tokens on Maybanks permissioned blockchain. "The initiative will assess the technical feasibility and operational readiness of executing on-chain transactions securely and in near real-time, while helping to shape the foundational design of next generation money rails and payment infrastructure," said the bank in a statement. Maybank president and group CEO Datuk Sri Khairussaleh Ramli said the bank is taking a holistic, pragmatic and inclusive approach to progressively expanding digital assets and tokenisation initiative into areas like investing, Islamic finance and supporting SMEs. "We continue to work on innovation to deliver tangible benefits for our clients like Yinson and the broader real economy, while upholding the highest standards of governance, security and regulatory integrity. Building on this first pilot, Maybank aims to pioneer a range of tokenised assets, including tokenised Islamic finance for businesses from large corporates to SMEs (small and medium enterprises), and retail customers, including wealth solutions. Through programmable money, the bank aims to empower SMEs by automating transparent payment flows from anchor clients such as governments and corporates, enabling payment transparency that can eventually unlock accessibility to financing. For wealth clients, the bank is exploring tokenised investment products, especially Islamic finance assets such as Sukuk and funds to enable broader participation through fractionalisation. Meanwhile, Yinson group CEO Lim Chern Yuan said the group is open to leverage innovative technology with the support of regulators and established financial institutions like Maybank. "Enabled by on-chain solutions, shorter settlement cycles to almost real-time allows Yinson to further manage working capital more efficiently and reduce foreign exchange exposure and transaction costs," he said.

KUALA LUMPUR: The FBM KLCI rebounds from the previous sessions losses, tracking firmer regional markets and buoyed by buying interest in banking and heavyweight stocks. The 30-stock index rose 8.85 points, or 0.51%, to 1,756.39, just shy of its intraday high of 1,757.15. Market breadth was positive, with 575 gainers outpacing 474 losers, while 549 counters traded unchanged on Bursa Malaysia. Turnover stood at 2.55 billion shares valued at RM3.06bil. Dealers said the rebound was largely in line with stronger regional cues, noting that selective buying in banking and index-linked heavyweights helped support the benchmark despite continued foreign outflows. Among the banks, Maybank climbed 26 sen, or 2.17%, to a record high of RM12.26, lifting its market capitalisation to about RM148bil. The counter has gained 2.7% over the past five days and is up 13% over the past month. Elsewhere on Bursa Malaysia, Hong Leong Industries advanced 76 sen to RM18.20, BAT gained 68 sen to RM6.15, and KSL added 23 sen to RM3.28. According to data on Bursa Malaysia, foreign investors offloaded RM38mil worth of equities on Tuesday. Local institutions and retailers acquired equities worth RM21mil and RM17mil respectively. On the forex market, the ringgit was quoted at 3.9170 against the greenback, appreciating 0.16%. The local unit fell 0.07% to 3.1031 against the Singapore dollar. MSCIs broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan rose 1.15%, with most regional markets ending higher. Japans Nikkei 225 climbed 2.28%, while South Koreas Kospi gained 1%. Hong Kongs Hang Seng Index added 0.31%, and Singapores Straits Times Index edged up 0.4%. In China, the CSI300 slipped 0.22%, although the Shanghai Composite Index rose 0.09%.

Market Review The FBM KLCI climbed 1.1% on Monday to 1,751.3 points, tracking Wall Streets positive performance supported by the SARA cash disbursement, while broader Asian market sentiment was further lifted by the resounding victory of Japans incumbent prime minister. Sectoral gains were led by Technology (3.7%), followed by Industrials Products & Services (1.5%) and Consumer Products & Services (1.4%). Among heavyweight constituents, 99 Speed Mart, Petronas Chemicals and Sime Darby emerged as top performers, advancing 4.8%, 4.8% and 3.2%, respectively. Market breadth turned positive from the previous close, with gainers outnumbering losers at 798 to 341, while 529 counters ended unchanged. Major Asian markets closed higher on Monday, as Wall Streets rebound boosted risk appetite and a landslide election victory for Japans Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi lifted regional confidence, supporting bets on continued fiscal support and a recovery in beaten-down tech shares. The Shanghai Composite rose 1.4% to 4,123.1 points, the Hang Seng Index gained 1.8% to 27,027.2 points, Taiwans TAIEX surged 2.0% to 32,404.6 points, while South Koreas KOSPI jumped 4.1% to 5,298.0 points. In China, gains were led by Information Technology (3.2%), Materials (2.0%) and Real Estate (1.9%), driven by Shenzhen JPT Opto-Electronics (20.0%), Shanghai Information2 Software (20.0%) and Changzhou Fusion New Material (20.0%). Meanwhile, South Korea outperformed, led by Industrial Services (6.4%), Electronic Technology (5.0%) and Communications (4.7%), supported by Samhwa Paints Industrials (30.0%), D.I Corp (25.5%) and Amorepacific Corp (20.3%). European stocks closed sharply higher on Monday, supported by banks, industrials and technology on positive corporate news and a broadly favourable macro backdrop. The STOXX 50 rose 1% to 6,058 and the STOXX 600 gained 0.6% to 621, both at record highs, led by UniCredits 6.4% jump on a Q4 earnings beat and strong dividend, while SAP and Adyen climbed 2.5% 5% and TotalEnergies, Siemens, EssilorLuxottica and Siemens Energy advanced ahead of earnings. US stocks started the week on a firm note, with the Dow reaching a fresh record high. In Mondays cash session, the Dow rose 0.04%, the S&P 500 advanced 0.47%, and the Nasdaq Composite jumped 0.9%, marking a second consecutive day of gains across all three benchmarks. Technology stocks led the rally, extending Fridays rebound after recent selling pressure driven by concerns over elevated artificial intelligence spending and potential disruption to traditional software business models. Megacap tech outperformed, with Nvidia up 2.5%, Tesla 1.5%, Microsoft 3.1%, Broadcom 3.3% and Meta Platforms 2.4%. Oracle surged 9.6% following an analyst upgrade tied to stronger AI-related demand. Looking ahead, investors are awaiting the delayed January employment report and upcoming CPI data for clues on economic cooling and the timing of potential policy easing. Macro Snapshots Corporate Snapshots Macro News US:Consumer inflation, labour expectations improve, Fed survey shows US consumer expectations for inflation and the job market improved slightly in January, according to a Federal Reserve Bank of New York survey. Households saw prices rising 3.1% over the next year, down from 3.4% in December, according to the median response in results of the New York Fed's monthly Survey of Consumer Expectations, released Monday. The probability consumers assigned to losing a job also edged down, while their perceived chances of being able to find a new one within three months improved to around 46%. Signs of a stabilising labour market paved the way for Fed officials to hold interest rates steady last month, after three consecutive rate cuts to close out 2025.(Bloomberg) US:IMF chief plays down the dollar's 'short term variations' The head of the International Monetary Fund (IMF) downplayed the dollar's decline over the past year, saying the US currency will probablyretain its  preeminent position. "We should not get carried away by short term variations of the exchange rate," the IMF managing director Kristalina Georgieva said in an interview on Monday with Bloomberg Television. "I don't see a change in the role of the dollar anytime soon." People "should look closely to why the dollar plays such an important role in the international monetary system," she said, citing "the depth and liquidity of capital markets in the US, the size of the economy and the entrepreneurial spirit of the US."(Bloomberg) US:Natgas Prices Extend Fall to Over 3-Week Low US natural gas futures slumped to below $3.20 per MMBtu on Monday, extending losses from the previous session and reaching its lowest level in over three weeks, as markets weighed the impact of warmer weather forecasts across broad areas of the country. KUALA LUMPUR: Here is a recap of the announcements that made headlines in Corporate Malaysia.

In a statement today, Maybank said the initiative reflects its continued focus on ensuring that access to financing translates into real economic activity, while making it simpler and more accessible for SMEs to obtain the funding they need to grow and invest. "As part of the new RM1 billion commitment, Maybank will introduce a new PG/-Islamic sustainable finance scheme for SMEs that have proactively transitioned their business operations towards more sustainable practices. "The scheme supports businesses that are making progress, recognising that sustainability is a journey while encouraging continuous improvement that delivers positive outcomes for businesses, the community and the environment, it added. Maybank said financing for other business needs is also available under the various PG financing schemes, including working capital, business expansion and supply chain, with financing amounts ranging from RM10,000 to RM1.5 million. It said SMEs may apply for the various schemes via Maybank2u through its SME digital financing platform. Maybank president and group chief executive officer (CEO) Datuk Seri Khairussaleh Ramli said Maybank is not just providing funding through its expanded collaboration with CGC, but is also enabling SMEs to invest with confidence, scale their operations, and build businesses that last. "Ultimately, we want to extend financing accessibility to a wider range of SMEs, while supporting more entrepreneurs across the country with solutions that go beyond banking, he said. Meanwhile, CGC president and CEO Mohamed Nazri Omar said the RM1 billion portfolio guarantee tranche reflects the corporations focus on deploying guarantee support in a more deliberate and targeted manner, in line with CGCs new five-year strategic direction. "By aligning portfolio guarantees across key financing priorities, including working capital, digital access, sustainability transition and supply chain ecosystems, CGC and Maybank are supporting SMEs at different stages of development through targeted financing solutions, he said. According to the statement, Maybank and CGC have supported SMEs through the PG/-i financing scheme since 2013. "As at January 2026, the collaboration has assisted over 35,000 SMEs, especially from the micro-SME segment, with total financing commitments exceeding RM11.8 billion over 85 tranches, the bank added. - Bernama

Market Review The FBM KLCI ended its losing streak on Friday as the local bourse marginally rose 0.1% to close at 1,733 points. Across sectors, Construction (0.4%), Transportation & Logistics (0.2%), Consumer Products (3bps) and Financial Services (1bps) were the only gainers, offsetting broader sector losses. Among index constituents, Axiata Group rebounded 2.1% to claim as top gainer, followed by Sime Darby (1.9%) and Gamuda (1.7%). Overall market breadth was still slightly negative as losers outpaced gainers 683 to 407, with 545 counters left unchanged. For the week, the FBMKLCI retreated 0.5%, with Technology (-0.8%), Industrial Products & Services (-0.6%) and Property (- 0.6%) offsetting gains from Construction (0.4%), Transportation & Logistics (0.1%) and Consumer Products & Services (3bps). Axiata Group (6.7%), Maxis (2.7%) and Malayan Banking (2.6%) were the top performers, while Gamuda (-9.9%), YTL Power (-6.4%) and YTL Corp (-5.7%) underperforming. Major Asian markets slumped further on Friday, as overnight weakness in US tech firms amid concerns regarding AI excessive capital spending towards software companies. The Shanghai Composite slipped 0.3% to 4,066 points, Taiwans TAIEX dipped 0.1% to 31,783 points, and South Koreas KOSPI dropped 1.4% to 5,089 points and the Hang Seng Index fell 1.2% to 26,560 points. Consumer Staples (-1.9%),Real Estate(-1.2%) and Communication Services (-0.8%) dragged the Chinese markets as tech stocks posted heavy losses, including Zhongji Innolight (-3.9%), Eoptolink Technology (-5%), Leo Group (-4.1%). Meanwhile, the South Korean markets saw Health Services (- 4.1%), Industrial Services (-3.6%) and Consumer Durables (-3.1%) emerging as sector laggards, with BYC Co (-14.9%), Daesung International (-14.0%) and Kyungbang Co (-13.9%) as the top losers. For the week, the Shanghai Composite, KOSPI, TAIEX and Hang Seng Index slipped 1.3%, 2.6%, 0.9% and 3.0% respectively. European equities closed firmly higher on Friday, rebounding from the previous session's severe falls to alleviate concerns about AI-related disruptions and excessive valuations in software sectors. The Eurozone STOXX 50 rose 1.2% to 5,998, while the pan-European STOXX 600 increased 0.9% to 617. For the week, the STOXX 50 and STOXX 600 added 0.9% and 1.0% respectively. Wall Street staged a broad-based rebound after a brutal tech-led selloff earlier in the week, with dip buying returning as concerns about near-term AI disruption and forced deleveraging faded as the Dow closed at a record high on Friday, up 2.5%. The S&P 500 increased 2.0%, while the Nasdaq CI rose 2.2%, boosted by a remarkable rebound in semiconductors, with Nvidia, Broadcom, and AMD each rising more than 7% as investors deliberately rebuilt exposure following significant drops. Cyclicals outperformed, with Caterpillar up 7.1% and Goldman Sachs up 4.3%. Despite Friday's rally, the Nasdaq closed the week down 1.9%, suggesting ongoing concerns about lofty valuations, significant AI capital commitments, and earnings execution risk. The S&P 500 ended the week 0.1% lower, while the Dow rose 2.5%. KUALA LUMPUR (Feb 4): Payments Network Malaysia Sdn Bhd, the operator of DuitNow QR payment system, has formally appointed Praveen Rajan as its chief executive officer, effective Feb 1. He succeeds Farhan Ahmad, who stepped down on Jan 31, the national payments infrastructure company also known as PayNet said in a statement. Praveen joined PayNet as CEO-designate on Dec 1, 2025, as part of a planned leadership transition. Praveen said he aims to build on PayNets existing strengths by executing with greater discipline and clarity, enhancing operational resilience and stability, and deepening engagement with industry players and partners across key sectors to advance Malaysias digital economy. Prior to joining PayNet, he was the chief consumer business officer at CelcomDigi Bhd (KL:CDB). PayNet, set up in 2017, is 35.5% owned by Bank Negara Malaysia. Apart from the central bank, 11 Malaysian financial institutions are shareholders in PayNet, including the countrys largest bank by assets Malayan Banking Bhd (KL:MAYBANK). PayNet plays a critical role in the daily lives of Malaysians and in the countrys financial system, he said. Over the past two months, I have worked closely with a strong and committed team, and I am stepping into this role with deep respect for the work already underway. Source: TheEdge - 5 Feb 2026

Foreign Inflows Revive Confidence in Malaysian Equities The FTSE Bursa Malaysia Kuala Lumpur Composite Index (FBM KLCI) delivered a strong performance over the past week, shrugging off regional caution as investors weighed fresh tariff threats from the Trump administration alongside the U.S. Federal Reserves first policy decision of the year. Blue chips opened the week with a powerful rally, climbing to levels not seen in more than seven years, buoyed by foreign fund inflows and the ringgits resilience against the U.S. dollar. Mid-week profit-taking in heavyweight counters temporarily pared gains, but the benchmark staged a late rebound to close the week firmly in positive territory. In the final week of January, the FBM KLCI had advanced 20.89 points, or 1.21%, to settle at 1,740.88. Strength in Hong Leong Bank, IHH Healthcare, Maybank, RHB Bank and CIMB more than offset declines in YTL Corp, YTL Power and Axiata. Trading activity was brisk, with average daily volume rising to 3.31bn shares from 3.02bn previously, while average daily value surged to RM4.07bn compared to RM3.13bn a week earlier. The index underwent a healthy consolidation after touching 1,771.25 last Tuesday, capping a five-day rally of 72 points driven by net foreign inflows of RM719.3mn. January as a whole saw foreign funds entering on 15 of 21 trading days, culminating in a cumulative net inflow of RM1.04bn. Malaysias robust fourth-quarter GDP, resilient trade data, steady foreign direct investment and ongoing government reforms provided the fundamental backdrop, while global investors unease with U.S. assets following the World Economic Forum added impetus to the search for opportunities in resilient Asian markets trading at undemanding valuations. Malaysias performance in 2025 was lacklustre, with a modest 2.3% gain that only outpaced Thailand and the Philippines, while other Asian markets surged between 6.8% and 75.6%. Sentiment has improved in 2026, with the FBM KLCI rising 3.6% YoY by end-January, though peers such as Singapore, Thailand and the Philippines have posted stronger gains. Indonesia, after a stellar 2025, has slipped into negative territory. This divergence underscores Malaysias potential appeal. Strong fundamentals, a diversified economy anchored by manufacturing, semiconductors, commodities, and a stable political environment, combined with a skilled multilingual workforce and strategic location, position the country well to attract renewed foreign interest after a huge net outflow of RM55.5bn between 2019 and 2025. At a consensus CY27 PER of 14.3x, valuations remain below the long-term average of 15.8x, with earnings growth projected at 6.6%. Externally, investors remain alert to geopolitical risks, particularly tensions between the U.S. and Iran. President Trumps warning over Irans nuclear program and Tehrans counterrhetoric have heightened concerns of a regional conflict. While such developments could trigger profit-taking ahead of the Chinese New Year (see our earlier report Healthy Consolidation Likely Amid Global Crosswinds, renewed buying may emerge if investors believe global tensions will ease, especially with Russia and China signalling support for Iran. Meanwhile, the appointment of Kevin Warsh as the new U.S. Federal Reserve chairman is being closely watched, though expectations are that central bank independence will remain intact despite his perceived alignment with Trumps low-rate agenda. Upcoming U.S. nonfarm payroll and employment data this week will provide further clarity on the sustainability of the Feds easing bias. Against this backdrop, we maintain our investment themes for Malaysian equities in 2026. Energy transition plays such asMALAKOF (TP: RM1.29), SAMAIDEN (TP: RM1.96)andTENAGA (TP: RM15.80), digital economy and infrastructure names includingCDB (TP: RM4.35), TM (TP: RM8.90), GAMUDA (TP: RM6.17), IJM (TP: RM3.15), MAHSING (TP: RM1.72)andSIMEPROP (TP: RM2.05), consumption and tourism counters likeALPHA (TP: RM0.37), F&N (TP: RM39.06), IHH (TP: RM9.67)andFFB (TP: RM3.22),fundamentally solid blue chips such asABMB (TP: RM5.30), CIMB (TP: RM8.45)andMAYBANK (TP: RM12.40),and select small and mid-cap growth stocks includingBNASTRA (TP: RM2.85), EXSIMHB (TP: RM0.30), PGF (TP: RM2.92)andSSB8 (TP: RM0.81)remain our preferred picks. Supported by fiscal reforms and steady foreign inflows, we retain our base case year-end target of 1,760 based on CY27 PER of 14.5 times, while acknowledging best case upside potential towards 1,850 as outlined in our Market Outlook 2026 report Turning the Tide. This target will be revisited following the conclusion of the fourth-quarter 2025 reporting season this month. Source:TA Research- 3 Feb 2026

KUALA LUMPUR (Jan 26): A strengthening ringgit will raise the appeal of Malaysian equities, benefiting index stocks and importers, said Public Investment Bank. Funds rotation would see capital being diverted from the developed markets into higher-growth and more fiscally-disciplined emerging economies such as Malaysia, the research house said in a note, as the ringgit appreciated past 4.00 per US dollar for the first time in more than seven years. Policy predictability and solid economic growth have attracted international investors into Malaysian financial assets, particularly the government bonds, the research house said. This has partially led to the strengthening of the ringgit in 2025, and the momentum is expected to continue into 2026. The ringgit gained as much as 0.8% on Monday to 3.9750 per dollar, its strongest level since June 2018. Kenanga Investment Bank expects the ringgit could touch 3.95 against the dollar before the year end while MARC Ratings said the local currency could hit 3.93 by the middle of this year. The ringgit is still one of the best performing major Asian currencies in the first weeks of 2026. The appreciation since the start of the year adds to the more than 10% gain against the US dollar in 2025. We continue to favour companies that rely on imported raw materials as their profit margins are likely to improve on lower translated cost of production as well as those that borrow extensively in USD-denominated currency, though this may also be offset by lower translated revenue, Public Investment said. Malaysias main stock index FBM KLCI, which tended to fall when the ringgit rose in the past, has also increased in recent months due to higher investment flows into the local market, the research house said. Public Investment favours KLCI constituents such as Malayan Banking Bhd (KL:MAYBANK), CIMB Group Holdings Bhd (KL:CIMB), Tenaga Nasional Bhd (KL:TENAGA) and Telekom Malaysia Bhd (KL:TM). Among consumer stocks, Public Investment likes Spritzer (KL:SPRITZER) and CCK Consolidated Holdings (KL:CCK). Source: TheEdge - 27 Jan 2026

Maybank held an Investor Day to highlight its new 5-year strategy. Highlights from the ROAR30 briefing are as follows: A Recap of the M25 Strategy To recap, Maybank launched its M25 strategy in 2022, a refinement of the original M25 plan, with a sharper focus on accelerating capability building for sustainable long-term growth beyond 2025. M25 was structured around five key strategic thrusts aimed at driving differentiation and long-term competitiveness. These include intensifying customer centricity, modernisation, strengthening Maybanks business presence and position beyond Malaysia, driving its leadership position in the sustainability agenda and becoming a global leader in Islamic Banking. Execution of the strategy was driven through 12 strategic programmes. To support delivery, the group committed RM3.54.5bn in investments, with approximately 77% allocated to technology. By end-2025, Maybank expects to see an acceleration in time-to-market of solutions by 2-4x, an increase in customer satisfaction by 10-20%, a digital customer penetration rate of 80%, employee engagement of 90%, and Next-Gen employees comprising 25-30% of the workforce. Loan growth is expected to rise at a CAGR of 7% and the cost-to-income ratio (CIR) of 45% by 2025, leading to an ROE of 11-12%. Encouraging M25 Progress Although Several Metrics Tracking Behind Target As of 3Q FY2025, execution progress has been encouraging. Group ROE for 9M FY2025 reached 11.5%, within the targeted range. The group also ranked #2 in global Sukuk leadership in 2025, registering 39% CAGR growth in Islamic wealth fees vs 24% overall wealth fee growth. Maybank also increased its midmarket portfolio to 14% of the Malaysian GB loan book from 12%, with deal count improving 29%. M25 performance has also uplifted targeted business segments across cash management, global markets FX sales, investment banking and insurance. Elsewhere, the group has mobilised some RM156.32bn in sustainable finance, significantly exceeding the original 2025 goal of RM80bn. In fact, Maybank has achieved all four sustainability commitments, leading to an MSCI ESG rating upgrade from AA to AAA. Digital customer penetration rose to 79%, close to the 80% target, alongside consistently strong customer satisfaction and employee engagement metrics. In parallel, Maybank also focused on improving asset quality, demonstrated by a reduced credit charge-off to 11 bps for 9M FY25, low gross impaired loans of 1.32% vs 2.23% in 2020, and healthy loan loss coverage of more than 110%, supported by RM2.5bn in management overlays. However, there were also some areas that tracked behind several of its key financial and growth targets set under M25. Cost efficiency remains the most visible shortfall. The CIR, which was targeted at approximately 45% by 2025, stood at 48.9% for 9M FY2025. Loan growth, which was guided at a CAGR of around 7%, registered only 2.6% on a YTD annualised basis as at 9M FY2025. Similarly, overseas income growth has materially lagged expectations, with actual growth of only 0.5% versus the targeted 7% CAGR for FY2022FY2025. ROAR30: From Capability Building to Growth Acceleration While M25 was largely about strengthening foundational capabilities, particularly in digitalisation, talent development, and operating platforms, ROAR30 marks a clear pivot towards supercharging growth. The new strategy is built around capturing four key ASEAN structural tailwinds i.e. rising regional wealth, improving intra-ASEAN trade flows, stronger foreign direct investment into the region, and accelerating global demand for Islamic finance. Pillar 1: Purpose-Led Growth Anchored on Value-Based Offerings Maybanks ROAR30 strategy is structured around three pillars. The first is anchored on a clear purpose of Humanising Financial Services through valuebased offerings. The group aims to deliver superior customer experiences, create positive societal impact, and play a more meaningful role in powering the real economy. Customer experience targets include achieving the top Net Promoter Score (NPS) ranking in Malaysia and top-quartile positioning in Singapore and Indonesia. On sustainability and community impact, Maybank has committed to mobilising RM300bn in sustainable finance by 2030, economically impacting three million lives, doubling social financing to RM24bn, and allocating RM100mn towards social empowerment initiatives. Maybank also plans to scale its support for the real economy, targeting SME financing of RM100bn by 2030 and a further RM100bn deployment into new-economy segments such as transition finance, the digital economy, infrastructure, technology, and semiconductors. Pillar 2: Building Businesses at Scale Across ASEAN The second pillar focuses on building scale and leadership across businesses where Maybank already has strong competitive positions to fully capitalise on ASEANs rising prominence. These include Islamic Finance, Wealth Management, Transaction and Payments Banking, and Regional Corporate & Investment Banking. In Islamic Finance, the ambition is to further lift fee-based income, expand Islamic wealth management and Halal ecosystem offerings, and sustain global leadership in Sukuk issuance, with clear targets to rank among the top five global Islamic banks and top four Takaful providers by 2030. Wealth Management is positioned as a key earnings accelerator, with aspirations to rank within the top five in ASEAN, deliver 20% annual wealth fee growth, and double profits over the next five years. In transaction and payments banking, Maybank aims to build a scalable regional platform across ASEAN, targeting a non-retail CASA ratio above 50%. At the same time, the group intends to defend and extend its leadership in capital markets, advisory, and loan syndication, with a goal of doubling fee income across both core and non-core markets. Pillar 3: Strong Foundations to Sustain Long-Term Performance Underpinning these ambitions is a Foundation pillar centred on people, technology, productivity, and capital discipline. Maybank plans to develop a future-ready, high-performing workforce, with 60% of employees classified as next-gen by 2030, alongside higher revenue per staff and greater enterprise mobility. Technology is a critical enabler, with RM10bn earmarked over the next five years, significantly higher than the RM3.54.5bn under M25, to fund core system modernisation, data and AI capabilities, and digital asset platforms, while accelerating customer-centric innovation through platform consolidation. Finally, ROAR30 also places strong emphasis on disciplined productivity and capital allocation, with a clear focus on improving balance sheet efficiency, cost management, asset quality, and capital deployment, reinforcing long-term resilience and sustainable shareholder value creation. Strategic Continuity Anchored on Core Strengths Despite the evolution from M25 to ROAR30, we note that several core elements remain firmly intact, underscoring strategic continuity. ROAR30 clearly builds on M25s ambition of establishing a values-driven platform, with regional connectivity continuing to sit at the heart of the groups strategy. This reflects Maybanks intent to fully leverage its ASEAN footprint to capture intraregional trade, investment, and wealth flows. Sustainability also remains a central pillar, with ROAR30 embedding valuesbased offerings as a unifying theme that extends M25s aspiration to be a regional ESG leader. Workforce transformation is another shared cornerstone, as the blend of human expertise with digital and analytical capabilities carries forward into ROAR30, alongside a renewed focus on building a highperforming, future-ready organisation. Core business priorities are similarly unchanged, with Wealth Management, Islamic Finance, and SME and Corporate banking continuing to anchor the groups growth agenda. Step-Up in Financial Ambition Under ROAR30 That said, the shift from M25 to ROAR30 represents a clear step-up in ambition. With the strategic horizon extended to 2030, ROAR30 places stronger emphasis on scaling the franchise, deepening regional relevance, and embedding long-term sustainability into the business model. This is reflected in more demanding financial targets, including a higher aspirational ROE of 13 14%, underpinned by an operating income CAGR of 56% and a core fee income CAGR of 67%. Balance sheet and funding discipline are also a priority, with targets to lift the CASA ratio to above 41% (from around 39%), sustain Net Interest Margin above 2.05%, and proactively manage credit portfolios to maintain a stable net credit cost of around 20 bps. On the cost front, Maybank is targeting a CIR below 47% by 2030, to be achieved through a combination of continued technology investment and cost optimisation levers such as productivity improvements, process re-engineering, attrition management, strategic procurement, and network optimisation. Overall cost growth is expected to remain in the mid-single-digit range. Financial Targets Broadly Achievable We believe Maybanks 2030 financial targets are broadly achievable, though execution remains a meaningful risk. The group has already demonstrated its ability to lift ROE from 9.8% in 2021 towards the 1112% range targeted for 2025, lending credibility to the longer-term aspiration of 1314% by 2030. This is further supported by Maybanks scale, strong domestic franchises, and strategic focus on higher-return, fee-driven businesses such as wealth management, Islamic finance, transaction banking, and corporate and investment banking. That said, delivery will be predicated on a sustained regional recovery, as balance sheet expansion and fee growth are closely tied to ASEANs growth dynamics. For efficiency, the higher CIR tolerance reflects a more realistic acknowledgement of elevated technology investment requirements under ROAR30. Valuation and Recommendation We maintain our earnings estimates at this juncture. However, following an adjustment to our risk-free rate assumption to 3.5%, we raise Maybanks target price to RM12.40 (from RM11.40). Our valuation is derived using the Gordon Growth Model, implying a PBV of approximately 1.45x. We reiterate our Buy recommendation on Maybank. Key downside risks include geopolitical uncertainties, intensifying competition, and evolving customer behaviour, all of which could weigh on growth momentum and execution outcomes. Source:TA Research- 21 Jan 2026

Market Review The FBM KLCI ended Tuesday 0.8% lower, slightly breaching the 1,700 psychological level to close at 1,699 points. The local bourse was dragged further by declines in heavyweights as global geopolitical and trade uncertainties continue to affect the markets. The broader sectors softened, as Financial Services (-1.8%), Technology (-1.1%) and Health Care (-0.9%) emerged as laggards. Among index components, QL Resources (-2.9%), Petronas Chemicals (-2.7%) and CIMB Group (-2.4%) were the top losers with notable losses among banks, namely Hong Leong Bank (-1.8%), Public Bank (- 1.7%), RHB Bank (-1.1%) and Malayan Banking (-0.9%). Overall market breadth remained bearish, as losers continue to outpaced gainers 783 to 295, with 554 counters flat. Major Asian markets closed Tuesday weaker, as regulatory tightening in China as well as global headwinds from US tariff threats and geopolitical tension further heightened cautious sentiment around the globe. The Shanghai Composite marginally trimmed one basis point to 4,114 points, the KOSPI slipped 0.4% to 4,886 points and the Hang Seng inched 0.3% lower to 26,488 points while the TAIEX outperformed, inching 0.4% higher to 31,760 points. Information Technology (-1.7%), Health Care (-1.1%) and Communication Services (-0.8%) pulled down the Chinese markets, as technology stocks led losses with Eoptolink (-5.1%), Guangzhou Haige (-6.7%), Zhongji Innolight (- 3.4%), and Zhejiang Sanhua (-1.2%) among the most significant decliners. Similarly, the South Korean markets saw Electronic Technology (-2.4%), Health Services (-1.1%) and Consumer Durables (-0.6%) weighed down the market as heavyweights, Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix dropped 2.8%, alongside Kia Corporation (-3.3%) and Hanwha Aerospace (-1.6%). Meanwhile, Producer Manufacturing (2.0%), Transportation (1.5%) and Commercial Services (1.0%) lifted Taiwan markets with Evergreen Aviation Technology (4.9%), King Slide Works (10.0%) and Ableprint Technology (5.2%) emerging amongst the notable top gainers. European markets closed sharply lower on Tuesday, extending the previous session's decline amid additional pressure from tariff threats from the United States. The Eurozone's STOXX 50 sank 0.6% to 5,892, while the STOXX 600 fell 0.7% to 603. Wall Street closed substantially lower, with the S&P 500 down 2.1%, the Dow down 1.8%, and the Nasdaq CI down 2.4%. Losses were driven by a surge in trade volatility when President Trump announced additional tariffs on eight European countries associated with opposition to US control of Greenland. Selling pressure increased as Treasury yields rose, with rumors that a Danish pension fund intends to reduce its exposure to US Treasuries, adding to risk aversion and impacting on rate sensitive and growth companies. Large-cap tech and semiconductors led declines, with Nvidia (-4.4%), Broadcom (-5.4%), and Oracle (-5.8%) all suffering significant losses as investors trimmed high beta exposure, while cyclicals and industrials also declined. Annual steel output declined 4.4% to 961m tonnes, falling below the one billion mark for the first time since 2019, with December at a two-year low of 68.2m tonnes. The energy transition that China leads relies heavily on aluminium, the lightweight metal used in renewables, power lines and electric car chassis. Steel is the backbone of construction, an industry dogged by the countrys years-long property crisis. China is the worlds biggest producer of both metals and both have been subject to output controls to varying degrees. Chinese leaders have pledged to "significantly" lift household consumption's share of the economy over the next five years, though they have not given a specific target. KUALA LUMPUR (Jan 20): Malayan Banking Bhd (KL:MAYBANK) has officially launched ROAR30, its new five-year strategic roadmap to 2030, with a key financial target of raising the groups return on equity (ROE) to between 13% and 14%, from the current 11.5%. Unveiling the strategy, Maybank president and group chief executive officer Datuk Seri Khairussaleh Ramli said ROAR30 is designed to position the bank to capture Aseans long-term growth opportunities. We believe that now is the time for us to supercharge our business growth and capitalise on Aseans growing significance, he told a media briefing. Our main objective is about driving shareholder value through continued improvement, while building the foundation to future-proof Maybank for the next decade, he said. Under ROAR30, the groups financial aspirations include sustaining a net interest margin above 2.05%, achieving a current account and saving account ratio of more than 41%, keeping net credit costs stable at around 20 basis points, and lifting overseas income growth to 5-6% by 2030. The bank also targets a 9% increase in non-interest income (NOII) over the next five years, driven by core fee income growth of 6-7%, and aims to raise its NOII ratio to 39% from the current 35%. ROAR30 is designed to deliver sustainable shareholder value by combining disciplined financial management with regional connectivity and long-term investments in people and technology, Khairussaleh said. He said the strategy rests on three pillars. The first is reinforcing Maybanks purpose of humanising financial services by delivering values-based solutions for customers while "impacting society positively and powering the real economy, Khairussaleh said. As part of this commitment, Maybank will mobilise RM300 billion in sustainable finance cumulatively by 2030, expand SME financing to RM100 billion, and channel another RM100 billion into new-economy sectors such as the net-zero transition, digital economy, technology infrastructure and advanced manufacturing. The second pillar focuses on building businesses at scale, with Maybank seeking regional or global leadership in four core areas: global Islamic finance, regional wealth management, transaction and payments banking, and corporate and investment banking. Khairussaleh noted that Maybank is already ranked second globally in sukuk by deal count, and value/ Islamic banking will serve as a prime mover for innovation, including initiatives such as Islamic Banking-as-a-Service in Indonesia. In wealth management, Maybank plans to build a scalable regional transaction and payments platform to support cross-border flows and embed itself more deeply as the primary bank for its clients. The group is targeting a top-five position among Asean premier wealth managers, leveraging its strong domestic franchise to deepen customer relationships across Malaysia, Singapore and Indonesia. The third pillar centres on future-proofing the organisation, including workforce transformation and technology leadership. Maybank aims to significantly increase the proportion of next-generation talent skilled in digital, data, IT and ESG to 60% by 2030, up from about 30% currently. On technology, the bank will commit RM10 billion over the next five years to investments in cloud, data and artificial intelligence, enhancing productivity, customer experience and resilience. Tech investment, we believe, will transform capabilities, but this will also be balanced with optimised cost management, Khairussaleh said. At the time of writing, Maybank shares were down 12 sen or 1.1% at RM11.04, valuing the banking group at RM133.38 billion. Over the past year, the stock has gained 7.4%. Source: TheEdge - 21 Jan 2026

KUALA LUMPUR (Jan 20): Malayan Banking Bhd (KL:MAYBANK) is set to invest RM10 billion in technology over the next five years. This massive digital push includes plans for a new banking app to replace the current MAE app, as the lender accelerates adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) to modernise its platforms and regional applications. At the unveiling of the banks five-year strategy entitled ROAR30, president and group chief executive officer Datuk Seri Khairussaleh Ramli said the plan will focus on scaling and strengthening Maybanks technology capabilities to drive long-term growth. The end game is to gain deeper insight and support our customers more effectively, but equally important is equipping Maybankers with the right tools to help customers achieve their goals, he said during a media briefing on Tuesday. The strategy will be guided by four priorities: establishing a resilient foundation, embracing new technologies such as AI and machine learning to optimise productivity, delivering best-in-class user experiences, and enabling new ways of working for employees, he said. Maybank has already spent RM1 billion on technology investments under its "M25" strategy, launched in October 2022. The latest planned investments are expected to help Maybank scale with speed and agility, expand its app ecosystem, and improve business outcomes, including shorter product development cycles and enhanced service offerings. The investments will also empower internal talent through upskilling and the adoption of advanced technologies. Future digital development will be designed at group and regional levels rather than for individual markets, with Indonesia likely to be the first rollout ahead of Malaysia and Singapore. Although no specific launch date has been set for the new app, Khairussaleh noted that retail-focused applications are already in development and updates will be shared later this year. We dont want to jumpstart expectations, but we think it should happen sooner rather than later, he said. All technology investments will be funded internally, with no fundraising required, Khairussaleh stressed. To balance the heavy spending, Maybank will implement group-wide efficiency measures, focusing on productivity improvements, workforce planning in view of upcoming retirements, stronger procurement discipline, and branch network optimisation. Source: TheEdge - 21 Jan 2026

KUALA LUMPUR: Maybank plans to introduce a new retail banking application to replace its existing MAE app, as part of a broader technology investment to enhance digital capabilities and strengthen customer engagement across the region. President and group CEO Khairussaleh Ramli said the new app is part of a RM10 billion investment in technology over five years. He said the new app is intended to leapfrog current capabilities and will be developed as a single platform to serve multiple markets and countries within the groups regional footprint. MAE is good, but there are certain capabilities that we need to put in place. We are now focused on developing for the group and the region. We want to build a single platform for multiple footprints and countries, he said at a press briefing in conjunction with the launch of Roar30, Maybanks new five-year strategy through 2030. Khairussaleh said the plan is to build one scalable platform for individual customers that can be rolled out across the region rather than developing market-specific solutions, to improve efficiency and enhance customer experience. He said Indonesia could be the first market for the rollout, given the importance of digital banking in the country, while Malaysia and Singapore might follow soon after. Khairussaleh said the new app would support the groups efforts to grow fee-based income through improved cross-selling of solutions on the retail side, while complementing transaction banking and payments capabilities on the non-retail side.

FBM KLCI Resilient Amid Uncertainty The FTSE Bursa Malaysia Kuala Lumpur Composite Index (FBM KLCI) delivered a mixed performance last week, swinging between gains and losses before ending on a firmer note. The market opened positively, shrugging off initial concerns over heightened geopolitical uncertainty following U.S. military action in Venezuela. However, trading soon turned volatile in the absence of strong domestic catalysts, with sentiment further weighed down by mixed U.S. economic data and escalating rhetoric from President Donald Trump. Caution prevailed until late in the week when renewed buying interest in key index heavyweights helped the benchmark recover lost ground. On a week-on-week basis, the FBM KLCI rose 16.78 points, or 1.0 percent, to close at 1,686.54. Gains in Nestle Malaysia, CelcomDigi, Maybank, 99 Speed Mart and Hong Leong Bank outweighed declines in Petronas Chemicals, QL Resources and Gamuda. Market liquidity improved, with average daily traded volume rising to 2.71 billion shares compared to 1.97 billion the previous week, while average daily traded value climbed to RM2.7 billion against RM1.86 billion earlier. The near 17-point surge on Friday was particularly notable, driven by local fund support that neutralised foreign selling pressure, while upbeat U.S. equitieswhere the Dow Jones Industrial Average and S&P 500 hit fresh highslifted sentiment further. The FBM KLCIs rebound reflects resilience, but sustaining gains will depend on domestic political clarity, economic momentum, and the global backdrop. Investors should brace for volatility as local politics, external demand, and geopolitical risks converge in the weeks ahead. The UMNO General Assembly, scheduled from Wednesday to Saturday, looms as a potential source of volatility. Investors are watching closely whether the assembly decides to contest the partys top two positions in the March election. According to UMNO secretarygeneral Datuk Dr. Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki, the party has received 662 motions from 175 divisions, with none suggesting withdrawal from the Unity Government or cooperation with PAS. This aligns with the presidents stance and reflects a strong support base, even if leadership contests emerge. On the economic front, attention is turning to Malaysias fourth-quarter GDP release. The Bloomberg consensus advanced estimate pointed to 5.4% YoY growth, but weaker-thanexpected November industrial production may trim the figure slightly. Industrial Production Index growth slowed to 4.3% YoY in November, below forecasts of 5.3% YoY and down from Octobers 6.0% YoY. On a month-to-month basis, output contracted by 1.1%, reflecting weaker mining and slower manufacturing expansion, though electricity provided a modest lift. While Novembers moderation narrowed momentum, Octobers strong surge and Decembers seasonal demand likely balanced the quarter, keeping the 4Q25 GDP growth above 5% YoY versus our forecast of 4.7% YoY. Externally, the U.S. labour market ended 2025 on a softer note. Nonfarm payrolls rose by 50,000 in December, below expectations and down from a revised 56,000 in November. Octobers figures were also revised sharply lower, underscoring fragility in hiring. The unemployment rate, however, fell to 4.4%, slightly better than forecast 4.5% and Novembers 4.6%, while average hourly earnings improved to 3.6% YoY. For the full year, the U.S. added 584,000 jobs, the weakest annual gain since 2003. These figures are unlikely to trigger immediate rate cuts at the January Federal Reserve meeting, but investors are closely watching December CPI data, expected to hold at 2.7% YoY. Meanwhile, Chinas December inflation data showed modest improvement. CPI edged up to 0.8% YoY from 0.7% in November, while PPI narrowed its contraction to -1.9% from -2.2%. Although this suggests easing deflationary pressures, domestic demand remains subdued. Markets will be monitoring Chinas December trade data this week, particularly in light of new U.S. sanctions imposing tariffs of at least 500% on nations trading Russian-origin uranium and petroleum products. At the same time, geopolitical tensions remain elevated. On January 9, 2026, Russia launched a large-scale overnight attack on Ukraine, deploying its new Oreshnik hypersonic missile against targets in Lviv. The strike hit critical infrastructure, including a major underground gas storage facility, escalating geopolitical risks and reinforcing uncertainty in European energy markets. Russia claimed the attack was retaliation for a supposed Ukrainian drone strike on President Putins residence but it appeared more like a warning to NATO allies, particularly after discussions in Europe about deploying peacekeeping troops to western Ukraine. The timing is closely linked to the seizure of a Russianflagged oil tanker by the US this month and can be seen as a retaliatory signal to Washington as well. The strike underscores Russias willingness to use advanced, nuclear-capable hypersonic weapons, raising the stakes in the conflict and complicating diplomatic efforts. President Trumps threats to take over Greenland also could unravel NATO and shift the global power balance as suggestions were mooted by France and Italy to deal directly with Russia over Ukraine. Source:TA Research- 12 Jan 2026

KUALA LUMPUR (Jan 7): Vantris Energy Bhd (KL:VANTNRG), formerly Sapura Energy Bhd, has secured two work orders from PETRONAS Carigali Sdn Bhd worth a combined RM1.4 billion for offshore transportation and installation works. In a filing with Bursa Malaysia on Wednesday, the group said the work orders were awarded to its wholly owned unit Sapura Offshore Sdn Bhd, under an existing contract. The awards relate to the Sepat Integrated Redevelopment Project offshore Terengganu, and the Belud South Greenfield Development Project offshore Sabah. Work on both projects is expected to commence in the first quarter of 2026, said Vantris Energy. The Belud South project is scheduled for completion by the fourth quarter of 2027, while the Sepat project is expected to be completed by the third quarter of 2029. Vantris Energy said the contracts will be executed by its engineering and construction (E&C) arm and will contribute positively to earnings from the financial year ending Jan 31, 2027 until the expiry of the work orders. Group chief executive officer Muhammad Zamri Jusoh said the contracts demonstrate Vantris Energys offshore transportation and installation capabilities, and its continued focus to deliver sustainable performance across its core businesses. These contracts prioritise opportunities aligned with our capabilities, regional strengths and risk appetite, he said in a statement. For its third financial quarter ended Oct 31, 2025, Vantris Energy posted a net profit of RM4.27 billion, as opposed to a net loss of RM293.06 million a year earlier, as it recorded a one-off RM4.47 billion gain from debt forgiveness following its financial restructuring. Nevertheless, the group still recorded an operating loss of RM97.07 million, compared with an operating profit of RM215.11 million previously, as operating expenses rose 14.9% to RM1.08 billion. Quarterly revenue dropped 14.8% year-on-year to RM982.79 million from RM1.15 billion due to weaker contributions from its E&C division, which saw topline dropping 30.5% to RM486.86 million on lower completion of ongoing projects while others were completed. The restructuring, under which the group issued new shares and securities to settle outstanding liabilities  reducing its total borrowings to RM5.7 billion from RM10.76 billion  was completed on Sept 26, 2025. It resulted in the emergence of Malayan Banking Bhd (KL:MAYBANK), CIMB Group Holdings Bhd (KL:CIMB) and RHB Bank Bhd (KL:RHBBANK) as the group's first, second and third largest shareholders, with equity stakes of 20.27% , 12.13% and 7.21%, respectively. Vantris Energys shares closed unchanged at 38.5 sen on Wednesday, giving the group a market capitalisation of RM880.63 million. The stock has declined 35.8% over the past year. Source: TheEdge - 8 Jan 2026

On fund flows, local retailers emerged as the largest net sellers (-RM60m; 2025: RM0.17bn, Jan MTD: -RM0.061bn), alongside foreign investors, who recorded a 9th consecutive session of net outflows (-RM20m, 2025: -RM22.32bn, Jan MTD: -RM0.252bn). In contrast, local institutions remained as the key net buyers (RM80m, 2025: RM22.16bn, Jan MTD: RM0.313bn). OUTLOOK Following a robust 75.9-point gain in December and persistent foreign outflows, the KLCI is likely to see a healthy profit-taking pullback, particularly near resistance in the 1,7001,722 range. With near-term market reactions to the USVenezuela tensions expected to remain muted, we continue to favour buying on weakness. Malaysias fundamentals remain supportive, underpinned by solid GDP growth (2025/26: 4.8%/4.5%), undemanding valuations (2026F P/E: 14.6x vs 5-year mean of 17.2x), a strengthening ringgit (8.9% vs end-2024), and healthy core earnings growth of 7.6% in CY26. These factors point to a constructive medium-term backdrop, setting the stage for a potential uptrend resumption toward 1,750 after a healthy consolidation. KLCI Technical View: Expect Stiff Resistances Near 1,700-1,722 Zones After a brief 14.8-pt pullback from 31 Dec2 Jan, the index regained upside momentum, closing up 10.5 pts at 1,680.5. A break above 1,685 (16M high on 30 Dec) could extend the uptrend toward 1,7001,722 (123.6% FE), though overbought conditions near that zones may trigger a healthy consolidation. Key support lies at 1,651 (20D MA), followed by 1,631 (50D MA) and 1,616 (uptrend line/100D MA) Source: Hong Leong Investment Bank Research - 6 Jan 2026

FBM KLCI Gains Tempered by ProfitTaking, Politics, and External Headwinds The FTSE Bursa Malaysia Kuala Lumpur Composite Index (FBM KLCI) moved sideways for most of the week showing limited directional conviction amid a lack of domestic positive catalysts. Meanwhile, investor sentiment also remained cautious while they awaited the release of minutes from the U.S. Federal Reserves December meeting to gauge the path of interest rates in 2026. Later in the week, profit-taking emerged among key index heavyweights, pulling the local benchmark lower. Week-on-week, the FBM KLCI dropped 7.34 points, or 0.44 percent to 1,669.76, with declines in Petronas Chemicals (-16sen), PPB Group (-36sen), YTL Power (-8sen), Press Metal Aluminium Holdings (-13sen) and TM (-14sen) outweighing gains in QL Resources (10sen), Petronas Dagangan (34sen) and Tenaga (12sen). Average daily traded volume last week edged lower to 1.97 billion shares from the 1.99 billion shares the previous week, while average daily traded value increased to RM1.86 billion versus the RM1.7 billion average the previous week. The FBMKLCI closed 2025 at 1680.11, about 38 points higher than the previous years closing of 1642.33. This was after a strong five-week rally that lifted it to a high of 1,684.53 from a low of 1,597.68. Thus, the index is expected to pause for consolidation as we are entering January with domestic political noises compounded by growing external risks. UMNO General Assembly and Domestic Politics to Exert Downside Pressure The UMNO Youth wing had a special convention on January 3 to gauge grassroots sentiment on whether the party should continue its cooperation with Pakatan Harapan. Following this convention, it was proposed that the party withdraw from the Madani Unity Government and instead partner with PAS. Senior leaders, however, stressed that such proposals must go through the UMNO Supreme Council and General Assembly before any decision is made. As such, these events set the stage for the UMNO General Assembly, postponed from November 2629 to January 1417, and UMNO leadership election in March that should be held every three years, with the last one held on 18 March 2023. In the last election, Ahmad Zahid Hamidi returned as President uncontested, after the UMNO General Assembly in January 2023 passed a motion barring contests for the posts of President and Deputy President. Thus, this development could prove pivotal for Malaysias political landscape. Adding to the political intrigue is the recent string of resignations by BERSATU leaders, including party president Muhyiddin Yassin, from roles within the Perikatan Nasional alliance, which also counts PAS as a member. The UMNO General Assembly in January 2026 will be shaped by four major currents. 1)First, the post-Sabah election strategy will dominate discussions.Since the assembly was postponed to avoid clashing with the 17th Sabah State Election, delegates will want to dissect BNs performance there and conclude whether UMNOs alliances with local Sabah parties helped or hindered results. Sabahs demands for greater autonomy in resource management and development will also be a central theme, as UMNO seeks to balance national cohesion with regional sensitivities. 2)Second, preparations for Malaysias 16th General Election will be a forward-looking priority. UMNO will use the assembly to strengthen its electoral machinery, refine grassroots networks, and modernise digital campaigning to appeal to younger, urban voters. Candidate selection will be a sensitive issue, with early signals about leadership succession and parliamentary candidates likely to emerge. The party will also focus on shaping its narrative around stability, MalayMuslim unity, and economic recovery, while considering alliance strategies with BN partners and possibly other parties to maximise its reach. 3)Third, internal party reforms will be debated intensely.Leadership challenges may surface, particularly after poor showing in Sabah elections. UMNO Youth and Wanita are expected to push for greater representation in decisionmaking, reflecting generational and gender shifts within the party. Grassroots strengthening will be emphasised, with calls for transparent funding, better training for local leaders, and stronger engagement with rural communities. 4)Finally, economic and social issues will be front and centre.Delegates will debate policies to address inflation, food security, and subsidies, pressing the government to ease household burdens. Youth employment will be highlighted, with proposals for entrepreneurship programs, vocational training, and job creation initiatives. National development will be discussed in terms of infrastructure, rural growth, and equitable resource distribution across states. In summary, the UMNO General Assembly will serve as a stage for fiery debates, policy resolutions, and potential signals of the partys future direction. Grassroots voices may intensify calls to abandon cooperation with Pakatan Harapan and revive the Muafakat Nasional alliance with PAS. Yet, as part of the current government, UMNO is unlikely to break ranks before the next general election, recognising that its 30 Members of Parliaments (MPs) cannot significantly shift the balance of power against a unity government backed by a comfortable majority of 153 MPs out of 222 and strong support from East Malaysia. Hence,any market gyrations over the next two to three weeks driven by speculation surrounding the outcome of the UMNO General Assembly may present a buying opportunityduring periods of weakness. Once the dust settles, greater clarity is expected, allowing the current government to advance its growth and reform agenda with renewed confidence. External Factors to Dominate Investor Sentiment On the external front, speculation over further easing in U.S. monetary policy, Chinas economic data and heightened geopolitical tensions are set to dominate investor sentiment in the weeks ahead. Market participants are keenly awaiting clearer signals from the upcoming Federal Reserve policy meeting on January 2728, which could shape expectations for the trajectory of interest rates. In the meantime, a series of high-frequency U.S. data releases, including ISM Manufacturing, ISM Services, trade figures, and the closely watched nonfarm payrolls report, will provide valuable clues on the health of the economy in December and the Feds likely stance. Across Asia, attention will turn to China, where the release of RatingDog PMI, consumer price index and producer price index for December will be closely scrutinised. These indicators are expected to shed light on the extent of deflationary pressures, driven by weak household demand, falling producer prices, and persistent industrial overcapacity. The readings will be critical in assessing whether Beijing may need to step up policy support to stabilise domestic demand while balancing its record trade surplus. Geopolitical Tensions Spreading Across the Globe The combination of ChinaTaiwan brinkmanship, South Asian diplomatic rifts, U.S. Venezuela tensions and Saudi-UAE conflict could trigger short-term gyrations. Yet, these may also create selective buying opportunities, especially in energy and defensive sectors, before clarity emerges later. China launched its largest military drills in years around Taiwan, dubbed Justice Mission 2025. The drills were widely interpreted as a rehearsal for invasion scenarios, testing U.S. resolve and regional security. January could see further escalation if Beijing continues pressure or if Washington responds with naval deployments. For Malaysia, this matters because regional instability could disrupt trade routes and investor confidence. Any escalation would likely pressure ASEAN currencies, including the ringgit, and weigh on export-oriented sectors. Meanwhile, diplomatic ties between India and Bangladesh have sharply deteriorated following the political instability, recent attacks on minorities and rising anti-India sentiment in Bangladesh. If substantiated, allegations that Pakistan is exploiting this conflict to amplify regional frictions could significantly heighten tensions across South Asia. Malaysias palm oil exports to this region could face volatility if political instability disrupts demand or logistics. Investors may price in risks to commodity-linked earnings. The U.S.Venezuela tensions are at their highest point in years, with Washingtons first ground strike inside Venezuela marking a dangerous escalation following weeks of U.S. naval operations and aerial interceptions of suspected drug boat.. Adding to the woes, the United States has launched a dramatic military operation last Saturday, capturing Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro and First Lady Cilia Flores. President Donald Trump announced that the U.S. will temporarily administer Venezuela to get the oil flowing, sparking global condemnation and raising questions about legality and precedent. This raises the possibility of a civil unrest in Venezuela and instability in Latin America. These moves have drawn criticism from Russia and China. Meanwhile, in the Middle East, the good relationship between Saudi Arabia and United Arab Emirates (UAE) has turned sour due to disputes over Yemen, Sudan, Somalia and Israel. In a latest twist, the UAE has supported separatist forces in southern Yemen, while Saudi Arabia backs the internationally recognised government. This has led to direct clashes, including Saudi-led coalition strikes on UAE-backed military vehicle. The rivalry could evolve in 2026. Uncertainties Could Increase Appeal in Blue Chips and Defensive Sectors Malaysia, as a net oil and gas exporter, stands to benefit from firmer crude prices. However, Bursa-listed companies that rely heavily on stable input costs could face margin pressures if geopolitical tensions escalate. For now, that risk appears contained given the global excess supply and readily deployable production capacity. According to the latest forecast from the International Energy Agency (IEA), the global oil surplus is projected at 3.84mn barrels per day in 2026, slightly lower than the 4.09mn bpd estimated in November. This suggests that while supply cushions remain ample, volatility in energy markets could still influence investor sentiment. Against this backdrop, investors may prefer blue chips and defensive positioning in sectors with predictable earnings, strong balance sheets, and consistent dividends. These counters can act as stabilisers while awaiting clearer signals from global energy markets and monetary policy shifts. Within power and utilities,TENAGA (Buy, TP:15.80), PETGAS (Buy, TP:20.58)andMALAKOF (Buy, TP:1.29)are supported by stable cash flows and regulated returns, making them reliable choices. The telecommunications sector, represented byCDB (Buy, TP:4.35)andTM (Buy, TP:8.90), offers recurring revenue streams and dividend yields, appealing to income-focused investors. In consumer staples, companies such asNESTLE (Buy, TP:125.90)andF&N (Buy, TP:39.06)benefit from steady demand that remains resilient regardless of macroeconomic volatility. The healthcare sector provides long-term growth prospects through essential services and uptick in demand from VMY2026, withIHH (Buy, TP:9.67)well positioned to capture this demand. Finally, among banks, selective blue chips likeMAYBANK (Buy, TP:11.40), CIMB (Buy, TP:8.45)andPBBANK (Buy, TP:5.15)continue to demonstrate fundamental strength, supported by diversified income bases and robust capital positions. Source:TA Research- 5 Jan 2026

As 2025 draws to a close, how has your investment performance been? Against the backdrop of global macroeconomic fluctuations, the KLSE index has risen by 2% year-to-date, becoming a key benchmark in focus. For investors seeking stable returns, "dividend monster" stocks have emerged as crucial components for achieving excess returns and strengthening portfolio resilience. This year, the following ten stocks in the Malaysian stock market have not only delivered strong share price performance but also offered dividend yields significantly above the market average, serving as pillars of steady returns in dividend-focused portfolios. Banking Stocks: Stable Dividends, Share Prices Hitting New Highs As one of the most familiar sectors to Malaysian stock market investors, banking stocks have continued to strengthen this year, offering substantial dividend distributions alongside capital appreciation. These financial institutions offer balanced investment value, and their sustained profitability can effectively enhance a portfolio's resilience even during broad market volatility. RHBBANK (1066.MY)has surged over 26.88% year-to-date, leading the banking sector, with its share price reaching a high of RM 7.8. Despite the significant increase, its dividend yield remains robust at approximately 5.58%, second only to Maybank among banking stocks. MAYBANK (1155.MY)has risen 7.99% this year and leads with a dividend yield of 5.96%a rare level even within the broader Malaysian market. Supported by strong revenue, profitability, and a high dividend distribution policy, it maintains its position as the largest bank by market capitalization. CIMB (1023.MY)andMAYBANK (1015.MY)have also entered the top ten annual high-dividend list, supported by notable share price gains and solid dividend returns. This reflects the ongoing prominence of the banking sectors value amid growing economic and loan demand. REITs: Income Highlights in a Rate-Cut Cycle Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) have long been favored by high-dividend investors. These funds to invest in income-generating properties such as office buildings, shopping malls, and apartments. During a rate-cutting cycle, such income-generating assets often attract more market attention. IGBREIT (5227.MY), which owns Mid Valley Megamall and The Gardens Mall in Klang Valleys Mid Valley City, has seen its share price rise over 31.6% year-to-date. It is the top performer among all Malaysian stocks with a dividend yield above 4% and a market capitalization exceeding RM 10 billion. KLCC (5235SS.MY)s share price has increased by 13.24% this year, with a dividend yield of 5.1%. Its key assets include iconic properties such as Suria KLCC and the Petronas Twin Towers. With the upcoming Malaysia Tourism Year 2026, these landmarks are expected to attract more visitors, further supporting performance. Utilities Sector: Steady Dividends, Lower Volatility Companies in sectors such as petroleum, telecommunications, and shippingincludingPETDAG (5681.MY),MAXIS (6012.MY), andMISC (3816.MY)have maintained stable dividend payouts this year, with relatively low share price volatility and steady gains. Against a backdrop of economic growth and steady industry development, their dividend distributions remain a key focus for investors. 2026 Outlook: Positioning for the Next Cycle Looking ahead to 2026, income-focused investors should pay more attention to the sustainability of dividends. Both the growth of corporate profits and the development of the macroeconomy are crucial. Kenanga has revised its fourth-quarter 2025 GDP growth forecast up to 5.0% from 4.0%, raising its full-year growth projection to 4.8% (compared to 5.1% in 2024). Further ahead, GDP growth is projected to ease to 4.2% in 2026 as external headwinds gradually intensify. REITs and banking stocks are relatively sensitive to interest rate changes. Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM) cut its Overnight Policy Rate (OPR) by 25 basis points to 2.75% in July 2025. Economists previously expected no further rate cuts until 2027. If further rate cuts occur in 2026, REITs could benefit from lower borrowing costs. For the banking sector, net interest margins may narrow in the short term, affecting profitability, while the long-term impact depends on whether rate cuts can stimulate loan growth. Looking ahead to the first half of 2026, which sector do you believe will offer the most sustainable dividend performance? Please leave your comment below! Loan growth in 2025 began sluggishly but rebounded following U.S. tariff adjustments. By October, business lending had expanded 5.3% YoY, with YTD growth outpacing 2024 levels of 2.8%. Momentum was strongest among SME-focused and mid-market banks, while wholesale and large corporate banking delivered more uneven results. Household loan demand remained resilient, though it moderated slightly to 5.4% YoY. The sector continues to lean towardmortgages, unsecured lending, and credit cards, and banks are expected to pivot further into high-margin segments in 2026 to protect profitability. Overall loan growth is projected at 5.7% in 2026, underpinned by 5.8% growth in consumer lending and 5.5% growth in business loans. Average Net interest margin (NIM) contracted in 9M 2025 to 1.98%, weighed down by BNMs policy rate cut and intensifying competition. Looking ahead to 2026, effective liability management and the pursuit of low-cost deposit growth will be critical to margin preservation. Larger banks are expected to continue expanding their current account and savings account (CASA) share, while smaller banks focus on targeted deposit initiatives to remain competitive. Our forecast anticipates a modest 3 bps YoY narrowing to an average of around 1.95% in NIM for 2026, assuming no further reductions in current Overnight Policy Rate (OPR) of 2.75%. In 2026, non-NII is projected to grow by 9.6%, supported by cross-sell strategies, trading and investment gains, and structural initiatives that leverage client relationships, market volatility, and specialised segments. Larger banks are expected to benefit from their scale and regional dominance in wealth management and capital markets, while smaller banks aim to stay competitive through partnerships, mergers and acquisitions, and digital investments. Banks are expected to balance strategic investments with disciplined cost management, as operating expenses rise about 6.0% in 2026 on the back of inflation and continued technology spending. Personnel costs are being contained through controlled hiring and redeployment into higher-value roles, while larger technology investments aim to strengthen long-term capabilities. Overall, the sectors Cost-to-Income Ratio (CIR) is forecast to remain broadly stable at 46.4%, as steady income growth cushions cost pressures and supports profitability Asset quality in the banking sector has remained resilient, with impaired loans trending lower, falling 3.8% YoY. We note of YoY improvements across both consumer (-0.6% YoY) and business (-7.9% YoY) portfolios, respectively. Loan loss charges stayed within guidance, though coverage ratios varied. Looking ahead to 2026, the outlook is broadly stable, supported by a resilient domestic economy, though banks are exercising greater caution in vulnerable segments. Gross credit costs are expected to remain contained at 2627 bps, while the average sector GIL ratio is forecast to hold steady at around 1.4%. For 2026, our stock-picking strategy centres on SME and business loan growth as a key driver of credit expansion, complemented by targeted high-yield lending to bolster margins and profitability. We see significant upsides cross-selling opportunities across wealth management, client services, and capital markets, which diversify income streams and reduce reliance on traditional lending. Larger, tech-forward banks are positioned to benefit from economies of scale and digital adoption, capturing efficiencies and sustaining competitive leadership in a rapidly evolving landscape. Against this backdrop, we maintain anOVERWEIGHTstance on the sector, withCIMB Group Holdings Berhad(TP: RM8.45, ),Malayan Banking Berhad(TP: RM11.40, ), andAlliance Bank Malaysia Berhad(TP: RM5.30, ) as our top recommendations. Source:TA Research- 31 Dec 2025

FBMKLCI Likely to End 2025 Firm, Poised for Gains in 2026 The local blue-chip benchmark FTSE Bursa Malaysia Kuala Lumpur Composite Index (FBM KLCI) posted a modest gain over the week as broader market activity remained muted with traders staying on the sidelines ahead of the Christmas and year-end holidays. Market sentiment was also aided by easing worries over stretched valuations in AI-linked counters, combined with expectations of further U.S. interest rate cuts in 2026, which in turn fuelled bargain hunting among selected index heavyweights. In the fourth week of December, the FBM KLCI gained 11.20 points, or 0.67 percent, to 1,677.10, as declines in Axiata (-7sen), YTL Corp (-5sen) and QL Resources (-8sen) were offset by gains in PPB Group (40sen), Press Metal Aluminium Holdings (26sen), Petronas Chemicals (9sen), Gamuda (12sen), and Tenaga (30sen). Average daily traded volume last week fell to 1.99 billion shares against the 2.55 billion shares the previous week, while average daily traded value declined to RM1.7 billion versus the RM2.41 billion average the previous week. The FBMKLCI eased last Friday after five consecutive sessions of gains, having reached a high of 1,678.85 midweek. This pause was hardly surprising, as we had anticipated window dressing activities nudging the index closer to the August 2024 peak of 1,684.68 before consolidating. While the rally could extend into the final three trading days of 2025, what is striking is the markets resilience in the absence of foreign inflows. Foreign investors have been net sellers almost every month this yearexcept for May and Septemberleading to a cumulative net outflow of RM21.5bn year-to-date. Looking ahead, January may bring short-term caution as profit-taking weighs on sentiment. Yet the broader structure remains constructive, with potential for new highs before political and macroeconomic factors trigger the next corrective phase. The ability of the index to recover despite persistent foreign outflows underscores the strength of domestic liquidity, which is likely to remain a key support in the months ahead. Seasonal trends provide some encouragement. Historical data from 1977 to 2025 shows equities tend to perform better between November and April, with a gain probability of 72.9% and an average return of 8.4%. This suggests the benchmark index could resume its upward trajectory after a brief profit-taking phase in January. Our long-held view remains intact that the major bull cycle that began from the March 2020 low of 1,207.80 is still alive, with room to test fresh highs ahead of the next general election, scheduled for December 2027 unless called earlier. This cycle mirrors the earlier uptrend that began from the October 2008 low of 801.27, peaked at 1,896.23 in July 2014, and eventually corrected into the COVID-19 low of 1,207.80. As we enter 2026, Malaysias equity market stands at the intersection of domestic resilience and renewed strategic momentum. Following an estimated GDP growth of 4.7% in 2025 (MoF: 4.0%4.8%), real GDP is projected to remain firm at 4.3%4.7% in 2026 (MoF: 4.0% 4.5%). This reflects a transition from recovery to stable expansion, driven by private consumption growth of 6.1%, robust capital formation, and a modest current account surplus of 1.5%2.5% of GDP. Corporate earnings of our stock universe are expected to grow at a CY2427 CAGR of 5.4%, supported by accommodative policies and resilient fundamentals. Based on a CY27 PER of 14.5xslightly below the CY21CY25 average of 14.7x and at -0.5 standard deviation to its long-term mean of 15.8xwe forecast the benchmark FBMKLCI to reach 1,760 points by end-2026. This points to modest upside potential, underpinned by improving earnings visibility, policy-driven liquidity, and eventual foreign inflows, though external shocks and geopolitical risks remain key headwinds. Our cautiously optimistic narrative rests on five interconnected drivers, which are timely reforms, robust foreign direct investments, strong consumption, corporate earnings recovery, and capital inflows supported by a dovish tilt in US monetary policy. These drivers form a reinforcing ecosystem where reforms create fiscal space that attracts FDI, FDI boosts productivity and wages, consumption strengthens earnings, and ringgit appreciation stabilises sentiment. Together, they position Malaysia not merely as an emerging market story, but as a reform-driven, consumption-led, globally integrated economy. The interplay of these forces makes Malaysias equity outlook in 2026 particularly compelling. Sector leadership ensures growth is distributed across industries, enhancing resilience and investor appeal. This virtuous cycle is likely to attract higher allocations to Malaysian equities, rewarding the combination of structural reforms, external competitiveness, and domestic strength. Key investment themes for 2026 include theenergy transition (MALAKOF, SAMAIDEN, TENAGA), digital economy and infrastructure (CDB, TM, GAMUDA, IJM, MAHSING, SIMEPROP), robust consumption and tourism (ALPHA, F&N, IHH, FFB), fundamentally solid blue chips (ABMB, CIMB, MAYBANK), andsmall to mid-cap growth stocks (BNASTRA, EXSIMHB, PGF, SSB8). These themes are supported by fiscal reforms and steady foreign investment inflows, offering investors a diversified pathway to capture Malaysias evolving growth story. (Please refer to Annual Strategy 2026: Turning the Tide for more details) Source:TA Research- 29 Dec 2025
