(The Edge/ Bursa Malaysia) Economy DOSM: Malaysias jobless rate steady at 3% in Sept, signalling continued labour market recovery The number of unemployed persons in Malaysia decreased further to 518,600 in September 2025, recording an unemployment rate of 3.0%, according to the Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM). In August 2025, the number of unemployed stood at 520,000. Chief Statistician Datuk Seri Mohd Uzir Mahidin said the positive economic performance had strengthened Malaysias labour market in September, with more people employed, fewer unemployed, and continued growth in inclusive regional development. In line with that, the labour force expanded further, increasing by 0.2% to 17.54mn persons (August 2025: 17.51mn persons). The labour force participation rate (LFPR) remained unchanged at 70.9%, he said in a statement. The number of employed persons in September increased by 0.2% to 17.03mn persons, compared to 16.99mn persons in August 2025. (Malay Mail, DOSM) Structural reforms on track; social protection, labour market needs boost - Bank Negara governor Malaysia is progressing at a reasonable pace in implementing strategic and structural reforms to achieve the nation's aspiration of becoming a high-income and developed nation, said Bank Negara Malaysia governor Datuk Seri Abdul Rasheed Ghaffour. Beyond fiscal and investment reforms, Abdul Rasheed said Malaysia needs to push ahead on social protection and labour market reform to strengthen the country's long-term resilience. "Overall, we are moving at a reasonably good pace. More importantly, there is a strong commitment to reform. Of course, we'd want it to be faster, if possible. But recognising and acknowledging the socioeconomic and sociopolitical realities, a step-by-step, careful approach might be what is needed. Ultimately, we also need to avoid policy flip-flops," he said in an interview. He stressed that if reforms were pushed too hard and fast, they might not get policy buy-in and acceptance. (The Star) B40, M40, T20 Classification Stays for Now Amid Ongoing Review, Says Govt The government will maintain the existing household income classifications of B40 (Bottom 40% income group), M40 and T20, said Acting Economy Minister Datuk Seri Amir Hamzah Azizan. When winding up the Budget 2026 committee stage debate in the Dewan Rakyat on Monday, Amir Hamzah said that while the existing classifications remain, a review of the categorisation is currently being carried out based on current needs. The proposed change in income classification was first introduced by former economy minister Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli last year, following the governments announcement to shift the RON95 fuel subsidy from a blanket to a targeted system. The initial plan aimed to help the government identify eligible recipients under the RON95 subsidy rationalisation programme. (The Edge, Bernama) Amir Hamzah: Five corridor regions attract RM87.56bn in total investments up to Oct 31 The countrys five economic corridor regions have successfully attracted total investments amounting to RM87.56bn in the first 10 months of this year, said Finance Minister II and acting Economy Minister Datuk Seri Amir Hamzah Azizan. Amir Hamzah said that between January and Oct 31 of this year, the five corridors successfully created 108,243 jobs. The countrys five economic corridors are under the jurisdiction of the Iskandar Regional Development Authority (IRDA), the Northern Corridor Implementation Authority (NCIA), the East Coast Economic Region Development Council (ECER), the Sabah Economic Development and Investment Authority (Sedia), and the Regional Corridor Investment Authority (Recoda). In addition, we are implementing a Corridor Authority (PBK) Impact Assessment, which aims to review the role and function, as well as the governance structure of PBK, expected to be completed in the first quarter of 2026, he told the Dewan Rakyat during the debate and winding up of the 2026 Budget. (The Edge, Bernama) New skills training bills will help Malaysians become more employable, says Steven Sim Malaysians will soon have more opportunities to acquire in-demand and specialised skills, as well as access better-paying jobs, following the first reading of two key amendments to the countrys skills development laws in the Dewan Rakyat. The move follows the first reading of the National Skills Development (Amendment) Bill 2025 and the Skills Development Fund (Amendment) Bill 2025. Human Resources Minister Steven Sim said the amendments aim to strengthen the countrys skills development system and prepare the workforce for a future economy driven by technology and specialised skills. The amendment under the National Skills Development (Amendment) Bill 2025 seeks to improve skills certification and develop a highly skilled workforce. It also aims to expand the National Skills Development Council to include strategic ministries and professional bodies, making TVET policies more inclusive. (The Star) Malaysia's Palm Oil Stocks Hit 6½-year High in October as Output Surges Malaysia's palm oil stocks rose for an eighth consecutive month to a 6½-year high by the end of October, as the biggest output in a decade outweighed a jump in exports, data from the industry regulator showed on Monday. Rising inventories in the world's second-biggest producer of the tropical oil could weigh on benchmark Malaysian futures, which are trading near their lowest level in four months. Malaysia's palm oil stocks rose 4.44% to 2.46mn tonnes in October from the previous month, the highest since April 2019, the Malaysian Palm Oil Board (MPOB) said. Crude palm oil production rose 11.02% month-on-month to 2.04mn tonnes, its highest since August 2015, while palm oil exports climbed 18.58% to 1.69mn tonnes, marking their strongest rate of growth in a year, the MPOB said. (The Edge, MPOB) Anwar: Cabinet to Decide Tuesday on Appeal Over Sabahs 40% Revenue Ruling The federal government will decide at a special Cabinet meeting on Tuesday (Nov 11) whether to appeal a High Courts decision that Sabah is entitled to 40% of the revenue collected in the state, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said on Monday. Anwar, who is also finance minister, said the government would first consider the advice of the attorney general before making any decision on filing an appeal. I have called the meeting for tomorrow (11 Nov 25). We will first hear the attorney generals statement on whether to appeal or not. For me, it is acceptable even if we choose not to appeal. But the final decision will be made after consulting all Cabinet members, he said in his speech at the Malaysia Digital roadshow . Anwar acknowledged that there are shortcomings and limitations in the matter, but hoped all parties would understand the situation. The court has directed us to negotiate, and I said that is acceptable and we will negotiate. After the (state) election (on Nov 29) and the formation of the new government, I will instruct that negotiations be conducted immediately, he added. (The Edge, Bernama) Amir Hamzah: Govt Efforts to Enhance Malaysias Diverse Economy a Success The governments efforts to enhance the countrys economic diversity are successful, considering the performance of the competitive economic sectors projected for the third quarter of this year (3Q2025), said Finance Minister II and acting Economy Minister Datuk Seri Amir Hamzah Azizan. Amir Hamzah said all economic sectors appeared robust in 3Q, based on the initial economic growth projection of 5.2% for the overall gross domestic product (GDP) released by the Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM). He said that the initial projections indicated competitive economic growth amid a challenging global economic environment, with the construction sector, for example, projected to grow by 11%, private consumption by more than 5%, and mining by more than 10%. (The Edge, Bernama) Australia Building Approvals Rebound in September Australia building approvals recovered in September, data from the Australian Bureau of Statistics showed on Monday. Building approvals increased 12% month-on-month in September after a 3.6% fall in August. Approvals for private sector houses rose 4.0%. Meanwhile, approvals for private sector dwellings excluding houses surged 26.0%. On a yearly basis, building approvals logged an increase of 15.3% in September. While approvals for private sector houses remained flat, that for private sector dwellings excluding houses jumped 55.2%. (RTT) Japan Leading Index Rises to 8-month High Japan's leading index increased more-than-expected in September to the highest level in eight months, preliminary data from the Cabinet Office showed on Monday. The leading index, which measures future economic activity, rose to 108.0 in September from 107.0 in the previous month. The expected score was 107.9. Further, this was the highest reading since January, when it was 108.1. Data showed that the coincident index also improved to a threemonth high of 114.6 in September from 112.8 a month ago. The coincident index measures the current economic situation. The lagging index climbed to 113.2 in September from 112.4 in the prior month. (RTT) Japans Takaichi Eyes Stimulus Package to Jump-start Growth Plan Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi aims to use her first stimulus package to jump-start the economy and initiate a new growth strategy through investment in key industries. A panel tasked to form a new economic strategy for the nation by next summer recommended that the prime minister use the package to kick off efforts to build robust growth through crisis management investment and growth investment, according to documents released following its first meeting. We need to boost private investment through measures that enhance the predictability of investment, Takaichi said Monday after the meeting. The panel said the government should focus on investing in 17 areas Takaichi deems essential for Japans economic growth. These areas include semiconductors and artificial intelligence, shipbuilding, the defense industry and critical minerals. (Bloomberg) Trump Says Close to India Trade Deal to Bring Tariff Rate Down President Donald Trump said he at some point would reduce the tariff rate on Indian goods, saying the US was getting pretty close to a trade deal with New Delhi. Right now they dont love me, but theyll love us again, Trump said. Were getting a fair deal. Trump later predicted the nations were pretty close to doing a deal thats good for everybody. The comments were the latest signal of a possible thaw in the trade dispute that has soured the relationship between the US and India. Trump earlier this year slapped additional tariffs on Indias exports to the US in part to pressure New Delhi to stop buying Russian oil, raising the rates on many Indian goods to 50%. That added tensions to an already contentious negotiation over what the US has cast as Indias high levies and other barriers on American goods. (Bloomberg) Trump Backs Deal to End US Shutdown as Senate Plans Vote on Bill President Donald Trump on Monday expressed support for the bipartisan deal to end the US shutdown, a key development that makes it likely the government reopens within days. We have support from enough Democrats, and were going to be opening up our country, Trump told reporters on Monday. Its too bad it was closed, but were going to be opening up our country very quickly. Senate Majority Leader John Thune said he expects the president to sign it into law once Congress passes the legislation. The Senate still must wind its way through potentially time-consuming procedures and House members must travel back to Washington to vote for the first time since Sept. 19. A final Senate vote to pass the spending bill is expected late Monday. It then goes to the House for approval, likely Wednesday. (Bloomberg) US Ports Facing goods Recession Amid Holiday Lull, Tariff Woes US import volumes are projected to slow through the year-end holidays and into 2026, as tariff uncertainty weighs on cargo owners and the outlook for consumer spending remains clouded, new data show. According to Descartes Systems Groups latest Global Shipping Report, the volume of US container imports slipped 0.1% last month compared with September, marking only the second October in the past decade to show a month-overmonth decline and a clear sign of importer caution. The October total of 2.31mn 20-foot equivalent container units, or TEUs, was 7.5% lower the year-earlier level and left the yearto-date tally just 0.9% higher than the total during the first 10 months of 2024. Separate figures from the National Retail Federation and Hackett Associates predict year-on-year declines in inbound container volumes of 14.4% for November and 17.9% in December. (Reuters) UK Firms Plan 3% Pay Rises in Coming Year, See AI Hit to Jobs, Survey Shows British employers expect to raise wages by 3% in the next 12 months, but some recruiters expect artificial intelligence (AI) to shrink their workforce, according to a survey that showed firms were worried about the impact of government tax plans on hiring. The Chartered Institute of Personnel and Development, a professional body for the human resources sector, said overall hiring intentions were around the weakest since the pandemic, and were especially low in the public sector. One in six employers expect the use of AI tools will allow them to reduce their headcount in the next 12 months. Of those, a quarter expect the staffing reduction to be greater than 10%, with junior managerial, clerical, professional and administrator roles expected to be the most affected. (Reuters) Eurozone Sentix Investor Sentiment Weakens Eurozone investor confidence weakened in November as the economy struggles to emerge from the slump, survey results from the behavioral research institute Sentix showed on Monday. The investor confidence index fell unexpectedly to -7.4 in November from -5.4 in October. The score was forecast to improve to -3.9. There is little sign of an autumn upturn in the eurozone, Sentix said. Both current situation and expectations indices declined at a similar rate. The current situation indicator posted -17.5, down from -16.0 in October. The expectations indicator dropped to 3.3 from 5.8 a month ago. "The persistence of this gloomy assessment of the situation is particularly indicative of the ongoing process of contraction," the think tank said. Any temporary glimmers of hope quickly fade away. (RTT) Source:TA Research- 11 Nov 2025

Market Review The FBM KLCI continued its momentum on Monday, gaining 0.5% to close at 1,627.4 points on the back of stronger ringgit and improved sentiment mirroring the broader Asian markets. The broader sector was positive with only three losers as Technology (2.0%), Consumer Products & Services (0.5%) and Financial Services (0.4%) were the top performing sectors. Among index movers, Axiata, Maxis and MISC led the gains by 3.9%, 3.0% and 2.9% respectively. Overall market breadth turned positive as gainers outpaced losers by 601 to 470, with 585 unchanged. Asian stock markets turned positive on Monday, on confidence that the historic U.S. government shutdown would soon end, while yields went up and the dollar kept its losses from last week. The Shanghai Composite inched up 0.5% to 4,018.6 points, the Nikkei 225 rose 1.3% to 50,911.8 points, the Hang Seng rose 1.6% to 26,649.1 points and the TAIEX inched up 0.8% to 27,869.5 points. In Shanghai, the top-performing sectors were Consumer Staples (2.6%), Health Care (1.0%) and Materials (0.9%). Among top gainers from the Chinese market were Guangdong Fangyuan, Jiangsu Biotechnology, Thinkon Semiconductor and Jiangsu HSC New Energy, all of which gained 20.0%. European markets climbed on Monday, rebounding from three-week lows set on Friday, as investors welcomed early signals that the unprecedented US government shutdown could be resolved soon and last week's tech bubble concerns subsided. The STOXX 50 rose 1.8% to 5,670 points, while the broader STOXX 600 increased 1.5% to 573 points. Across the sectors in the STOXX 50, almost all closed higher with Financials (2.7%), Industrials (2.2%) and Consumer Discretionary (2.0%) leading gains. A surge of positive corporate announcements boosted optimism even more. Commerzbank rose 6.2% as Deutsche Bank upgraded it to "Buy". Siemens Energy rose 4.6% following a similar rating upgrade from Jefferies. Diageo rose 5.7% after appointing former Tesco CEO Dave Lewis as its new CEO. Wall Street began the week strongly, with the S&P 500 rising 1.5%, the Nasdaq up 2.3%, and the Dow inched up 0.8% as the Senate advanced a procedural vote that brings a budget deal to reopen the government and end the 40-day shutdown closer to passage. Among top sectoral gains in the S&P 500 were led by Information Technology (2.7%), Communication Services (2.5%) and Consumer Discretionary (1.5%). AI-related technology names led the rally, with Nvidia (5.8%), Palantir (8.8%), AMD (4.5%), and Micron (6.5%) among the top gainers as investors returned to high-growth positions. MAYBANK has continued to strengthen its governance and integrity framework with several landmark initiatives that reflect its long-standing commitment to ethical conduct and accountability - symbolising its enduring commitment to embedding integrity as a cornerstone of its culture, operations and business practices. The group announced these achievements during the launch of Maybanks Integrity Day 2025, themed See It. Speak It. Live It, officiated by Maybank chairman Tan Sri Zamzamzairani Mohd Isa, along with president and group chief executive officer Datuk Seri Khairussaleh Ramli, as well as group chief compliance officer and chief integrity and governance officer Yiow Inn Sann. The event was witnessed by Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission deputy chief commissioner Datuk Azmi Kamaruzaman and Sirim Bhd chairman Datuk Khairol Anuar Mohamad Tawi. Key milestones include the successful completion of the ISO 37001 Anti-Bribery Management System (ABMS) Certification across all Maybank entities in Malaysia and overseas. This internationally recognised certification reaffirms the banks robust systems and processes to prevent bribery and corruption, ensuring transparency and integrity are upheld in every aspect of its operations. Complementing this is the launch of Maybanks Organisational Anti-Corruption Plan (OACP), which sets out clear strategies and initiatives to prevent corruption, promote integrity and reinforce a culture of ethics across the Group. Developed in alignment with the National Anti-Corruption Strategy (NACS), the OACP strengthens Maybanks accountability to both internal and external stakeholders. Meanwhile, back in 2011, the Maybank board and executive committee took a significant step by signing the Corporate Integrity Pledge, demonstrating their commitment to upholding integrity, fighting corruption and fostering ethical business practices. This pledge served as a strong declaration that integrity is a core, non-negotiable value at Maybank. Further reinforcing this commitment was the recitation of the Corruption Free Pledge (Ikrar Bebas Rasuah) by board members and senior management at the event, before proceeding to sign it  underscoring the organisations dedication to maintaining integrity at all levels. Speaking at the event, Zamzamzairani said that these measures reflect Maybanks belief that integrity is not just a policy statement but a lived value, embedded across its culture  demonstrated through the discipline, accountability and actions of every employee. At Maybank, integrity is not just a principle we talk about, but a standard by which we measure ourselves every day. It guides our decisions, strengthens the trust our stakeholders place in us and ensures that we grow responsibly and sustainably. Integrity is what defines us as a whole and is the foundation upon which we will continue to build a future of transparency, accountability and trust across the region while creating meaningful impact for all our stakeholders, he added. The Maybank Integrity Day 2025, held over two days from Oct 2 to 3, also featured a series of sharing sessions, interactive booths and employee engagement activities designed to strengthen awareness of ethical conduct, empower employees to make values-driven decisions and reinforce Maybanks corporate governance practices.

KUALA LUMPUR (Nov 6): NCT Group of Companies said it launched its maiden RM1 billion sukuk wakalah programme on Thursday. Given the large stake in the stock by institutions, Malayan Banking Berhad's stock price might be vulnerable to their trading decisions The top 2 shareholders own 52% of the company Analyst forecasts along with ownership data serve to give a strong idea about prospects for a business We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free. A look at the shareholders of Malayan Banking Berhad (KLSE:MAYBANK) can tell us which group is most powerful. We can see that institutions own the lion's share in the company with 68% ownership. Put another way, the group faces the maximum upside potential (or downside risk). Since institutional have access to huge amounts of capital, their market moves tend to receive a lot of scrutiny by retail or individual investors. As a result, a sizeable amount of institutional money invested in a firm is generally viewed as a positive attribute. In the chart below, we zoom in on the different ownership groups of Malayan Banking Berhad. View our latest analysis for Malayan Banking Berhad Institutional investors commonly compare their own returns to the returns of a commonly followed index. So they generally do consider buying larger companies that are included in the relevant benchmark index. We can see that Malayan Banking Berhad does have institutional investors; and they hold a good portion of the company's stock. This implies the analysts working for those institutions have looked at the stock and they like it. But just like anyone else, they could be wrong. If multiple institutions change their view on a stock at the same time, you could see the share price drop fast. It's therefore worth looking at Malayan Banking Berhad's earnings history below. Of course, the future is what really matters. Institutional investors own over 50% of the company, so together than can probably strongly influence board decisions. Hedge funds don't have many shares in Malayan Banking Berhad. Permodalan Nasional Berhad is currently the company's largest shareholder with 41% of shares outstanding. Meanwhile, the second and third largest shareholders, hold 11% and 5.4%, of the shares outstanding, respectively. To make our study more interesting, we found that the top 2 shareholders have a majority ownership in the company, meaning that they are powerful enough to influence the decisions of the company. While studying institutional ownership for a company can add value to your research, it is also a good practice to research analyst recommendations to get a deeper understand of a stock's expected performance. There are plenty of analysts covering the stock, so it might be worth seeing what they are forecasting, too.

Bank stocks in Bursa Malaysia play a vital role in shaping the local market and often serve as a reflection of the countrys overall economic health. These financial giants are well-regarded for their solid fundamentals, consistent dividend payouts, and resilience during market uncertainties. According to RHB Investment Bank, the sector remains fundamentally strong, with PATMI growth projected to rebound to 5% in 2026F, up from 2% in 2025F. This outlook reinforces the banking sectors stability and its continued appeal to long-term investors. Traditionally, bank stocks are known for providing steady long-term returns rather than rapid capital gains. However, one of their biggest attractions lies in their reliable and attractive dividend payouts, making them a core choice for investors seeking regular income. To help investors better evaluate these income opportunities, StockTipRatings.com provides exclusive forward dividend yield data, allowing for more informed investment decisions. This metric is calculated by dividing the average projected dividend per share (DPS)compiled from all analyst reports covering each companyby the companys current share price. Unlike the current dividend yield, which only reflects dividends paid in the past, the forward dividend yield offers a forward-looking perspective by capturing what investors may potentially earn in the future. This makes it a valuable tool for assessing expected income potential based on analysts dividend forecasts. Based on this data, StockTipRatings.com has compiled a list of the Top 5 bank stocks with the highest forward dividend yields, most of which are ratedBuyby analysts across the market. RHB Bank Malaysias fifth-largest bank by market capitalization, offers a forward dividend yield of6.6%, the highest among the five. It has received 12 Buy and 1 Hold ratings from 13 analysts, with a consensus target price ofRM7.48, implying a 12.1% upside from its current price of RM6.67. It trades at a forward P/E of 9.2x. Based on STR AI Analysis, RHB Banks strengths include a long-term bancassurance agreement (worth up to RM1.6 billion) that enhances recurring non-interest income and distribution reach. A strong capital buffer (CET1 16%), stable loan growth, and rising current account savings account (CASA) support its resilience and dividend payouts. Key risks include potential asset-quality deterioration, provisioning needs from SME and regional exposures, and external macro or tariff-related pressures from the US. Malayan Banking- Malaysias largest bank, has received 11 Buy and 4 Hold ratings from 15 analysts, with a consensus target price ofRM11.13,implying a 12.9% upside from RM9.86. The bank offers a forward dividend yield of5.8%and trades at a forward P/E of 11.5x. STR AI Analysis highlights that Maybank Banking maintains strong capital, liquidity, and provisioning buffers (CET1 14.715.5%, healthy liquidity coverage ratio, and RM2 billion overlay). Its diversified non-interest income streams (treasury, wealth management, andinsurance) help cushion earnings amid net interest margin (NIM) and loan headwinds. Nonetheless, asset-quality risks from Indonesia, SME, auto, and Greater China exposures could lift credit costs and pressure earnings. CIMB Group Holdings Malaysias third-largest bank, has received 15 Buy and 1 Hold rating from 16 analysts, with a consensus target price ofRM8.41, implying a 16.8% upside from RM7.20. Its forward dividend yield stands at5.7%, and it trades at a forward P/E of 10x. CIMB Group Holdings demonstrates healthy capital and asset quality (CET1 14.7%, ROE 11%, gross impaired loans (GIL) 2.1%). Strong funding resilience and CASA growth continue to underpin liquidity, loan expansion, and funding-cost stability. However, regional and FX risks  particularly in Indonesia  may weigh on earnings. Public Bank- Malaysias second-largest bank, has 12 Buy and 2 Hold ratings from 14 analysts, with a consensus target price ofRM5.08, implying a 20.7% upside from RM4.21. The bank targets a dividend payout ratio of about 60%, translating to a forward dividend yield of4.8%, and trades at a forward P/E of 11.4x. STR AI Analysis notes that Public Bank boasts very low impaired loans, high coverage ratios, and a strong capital position (CET1 14%, plus an additional RM1 billion management overlay). Its revenue is well diversified, driven by steady domestic loan growth of 56% and rising non-interest income from insurance, wealth management, and trading. Potential risks include shareholder overhang, possible LPI sell-downs, and governance or legal costs that could raise its cost of equity. Hong Leong Bank- A subsidiary ofHong Leong Financial Groupand Malaysias fourth-largest bank, enjoys unanimous Buy ratings from all 12 analysts, with a consensus target price ofRM24.24, implying an 18.2% upside from RM20.50. Its forward dividend yield of4.8%and its forward P/E is 9.4x. According to STR AI Analysis, Hong Leong Bank demonstrates above-industry loan growth and rising CASA, helping to control funding costs and support net interest income stability. The bank also maintains an exceptionally low GIL of 0.54% and CET1 of 13%, providing both a stress buffer and capital optimization flexibility. Key risks include earnings volatility from its Bank of Chengdu associate, translation losses, and broader China exposure. RHB Bank leads the pack with the highest forward dividend yield (6.6%), lowest valuation (P/E 9.2x), and strongest capital ratio (CET1 16%), making it the most attractive play for both income and value investors, according to StockTipRatings.com. On the other hand, Public Bank and Hong Leong Bank offer the lowest forward dividend yield of 4.8% among the five stocks.

Asian Markets Climb Amid Easing Tensions and Policy Optimism Asian markets traded higher on Tuesday, buoyed by hopes of easing trade tensions between the world's two largest economies and growing optimism over potential interest rate cuts, which lifted investor sentiment. Expectations that U.S. President Donald Trump will meet with Chinese President Xi Jinping later this month during a regional summit have raised hopes for an easing of trade tensions between the world's two biggest economies. Separately, investor sentiment is also buoyed by expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut rates by another quarter-point at its late October meeting. While upbeat results from several major firms lent support to the market, lingering concerns about stretched valuations tempered broader gains. Tech stocks also took a bit of a hit as worries about US-China trade tensions moved to the back burner even as the two countries prepared the ground for negotiations to resume. A lack of major U.S. economic data due in part to the shutdown may also have kept some traders on the sidelines ahead of the release of closely watched consumer price inflation data on Friday. "We want RCEP to serve not just the interests of large multinational corporations, but also to deliver concrete benefits to our small and medium enterprises (SMEs), workers, and communities," he said during the closing of the DHL GoTrade Summit 2025. The 5th RCEP Leaders Meeting is scheduled for Oct 27 and will be held on the sidelines of the 47th Asean Summit, taking place from Oct 26 to 28. (The Edge, Bernama) Car Sales Dip in September, Rebound Expected in October  MAA New vehicle sales in Malaysia fell in September, compared with August, amid fewer working days and a cautious consumer mood ahead of the Budget 2026 announcement, according to the Malaysian Automotive Association (MAA). Total industry volume came in at 58,490 units, just 0.2% higher than the 58,351 units recorded in the same month last year, the trade body said in a statement. However, sales were down 22% month-on-month compared with Augusts 74,479 units, reflecting the impact of four public holidays during the month and a wait-and-see attitude among buyers regarding details of the governments petrol subsidy rationalisation plans. For the first nine months of 2025, total industry sales dipped 3% to 579,336 units from 595,883 units in the same period last year. (The Edge, MAA) Moodys: Asia-Pacific Economy to Expand 3.8% in 2025 Asia-Pacifics (Apac) gross domestic product (GDP) is forecast to grow 3.8% in 2025, but will slow to 3.3% in 2026 and creep to a still-soft 3.5% in 2027, according to Moodys Analytics Inc. In a statement Tuesday, the financial intelligence and analytical tools provider said the numbers are well below its forecasts in late 2024, before United States President Donald Trumps return to the White House upended global trade expectations. They also mark a noticeable step down from the regions 4.0% expansion in 2024, it said. It highlighted that growth in the first half of 2025 (1H2025) was better than expected, with exporters rushing to fill orders before US tariff hikes took effect. But with higher tariffs now baked in, growth in 2H2025 will be slower. That drag will extend into 2026 and beyond, said Moodys Analytics. Exports powered the regions early-2025 resilience, as manufacturers shipped goods to the US at breakneck speed ahead of new tariffs, causing export and industrial production figures to soar above their 20232024 trend. (The Edge, Moodys) IMF Says Risks to Middle East Outlook Tilted to Downside on Global Uncertainty The International Monetary Fund (IMF), which raised its 2025 growth outlook for the Middle East and North Africa (Mena) last week, has said that risks for the region are still tilted to the downside, despite a recent improvement in geopolitical tensions. The IMF has lifted its 2025 gross domestic product (GDP) growth forecast for the Mena region to 3.3%, from 2.6% it projected in May. "The outlook this year reflected a resilience, despite the fact that we live in elevated global uncertainty and a situation where trade tariffs affected most of the world and the geopolitical tensions are still there," Jihad Azour, director of the IMF's Middle East and Central Asia department, told Reuters. Regional oil exporters are benefitting from higher oil production, as well as increased public investment and implementation of structural reforms as part of economic diversification efforts. Lower commodity prices, a rebound in tourism and stronger remittances have helped improve growth among oil importing states, with improved access to financial markets and moderating inflation providing support. (Reuters) China's New Home Prices Fall at Fastest Pace in 11 Months China's new home prices fell at the fastest pace in 11 months in September, worsening the property sector's drag on broader economic growth as policymakers struggle to revive the flailing market. Persistent property market weakness is weighing on consumer confidence and sapping household spending, building the case for policymakers to step up support to shore up growth amid global trade threats. New home prices fell 0.4% month-on-month, following a 0.3% fall in August, according to calculations by Reuters based on National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) data released on Monday. Year-on-year, prices fell 2.2% in September versus a 2.5% drop in August. (Reuters) Koreas Early Trade Data Show Robust Exports Despite Tariffs South Koreas early exports advanced in October, aided by resilient semiconductor demand even as US tariffs weighed on momentum and holidays created distortions. The value of shipments adjusted for differences in the number of working days increased 9.7% from a year earlier in the first 20 days of October, according to data released Tuesday by the customs office. That compared with a revised 6.1% drop reported for the full month of September. Unadjusted shipments fell 7.8%, while overall imports slipped 2.3%, resulting in a trade deficit of $2.8bn. The rebound came even though there were fewer working days in the first 20 days of October compared to last year due to South Koreas Chuseok holidays, which ran from Oct. 3-9. Semiconductor exports jumped 20.2%, while vessel shipments climbed 11.7%. Auto exports, meanwhile, slumped 25% as higher US duties began to weigh on the sector. (Bloomberg) Fed Still Poised to Cut Rates, But Worries Mount Over US Data Vacuum The Federal Reserve (Fed) will go into a policy meeting next week with its view of the economy obscured by a US government shutdown that has suspended the release of key data, a less-than-ideal situation for policymakers divided over which risks deserve the most attention. Official employment data hasn't been released since the shutdown of the federal government began on Oct 1, but what information that remains available points to still-weak job growth. The Fed's own economic field reporting, still underway at the self-funded central bank, showed possible cracks in consumer spending, and recent business confidence surveys pointed to a dip. Yet businesses are also warning of coming price increases at a time when inflation is lodged above the Fed's 2% target, estimates of overall economic growth are getting upgraded as the scope of business investment becomes clear, and economists have begun pointing to a possible economic jolt next year as new tax laws, including exclusions for tips and overtime income, boost households' refunds. (Reuters) Trump Sees Successful Xi Meeting, But Allows It Might Not Happen US President Donald Trump predicted an upcoming meeting with his Chinese counterpart, Xi Jinping, would yield a good deal on trade  while also conceding that the highly anticipated talks may not happen. I have a great relationship with President Xi. I expect to be able to make a good deal with him, Trump said Tuesday during a lunch with Republican lawmakers in the White House Rose Garden. I want him to make a good deal for China  but its got to be fair. While Trump foresaw the sit-down as being very successful, he said the possibility remains that it could fail to materialize. The two leaders are scheduled to meet later this month at the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation summit in South Korea. The date and time for the anticipated leaders meeting has not yet been announced, though the US leader said it would be in about two weeks. (Bloomberg) Trump Official Warns China Against Penalising Companies Investing in US The Trump administration warned China not to retaliate against foreign companies helping the US to develop critical industries, after Beijing last week sanctioned the US units of a South Korean shipping giant over its plans to invest in the American maritime sector. Chinas recent retaliatory actions against private companies across the globe is part of a broader pattern of economic coercion to influence American politics and control global supply chains by discouraging foreign companies from investing in Americas shipbuilding and other critical industries, US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said in a statement. Greers warning is the latest step in a long-standing series of maritime disputes between the US and China, which accounts for more than half of the worlds shipbuilding and has sought in recent years to increase its control of the critical South China Sea. The battle has implications for the global economy, as vessels are responsible for moving more than 80% of international trade. (Bloomberg) UK Budget Deficit Overshoots Estimate The UK budget deficit surpassed the official estimate in the financial year to September, reaching the second highest level on record due to higher debt costs, data from the Office for National Statistics showed on Tuesday. In the first half of the year, public sector net borrowing rose by GBP11.5bn from the last year to GBP99.8bn. The borrowing reached the second-highest April to September level since monthly records began in 1993, after that of 2020. Moreover, this was above the Office for Budget Responsibility's estimate of GBP92.6bn. In September alone, public sector net borrowing increased to GBP 0.2bn from GBP 18.6bn in the previous year. This was the highest September borrowing since 2020. Data showed that current receipts grew 7.6% to GBP523.7bn. At GBP557.2bn, current expenditure was 9.3% more than in same period last year. Within expenditure, interest payable on central government debt increased GBP14.4bn to GBP59.5bn. At the end of September, public sector net debt excluding public sector banks reached 95.3% of gross domestic product, the ONS said. (Reuters) Source:TA Research- 22 Oct 2025

Malaysias banks are resilient, with BNMs latest stress test reaffirming their strength despite elevated geopolitical and trade-related risks in 1HCY25, supported by sound financial system.Both household (HH) and business (Biz) segments stayed healthy. We maintain a positive view on the sector, supported by attractive valuations and a compelling c.5% dividend yield. Additionally, KLFIN continues to lag the KLCI YTD, offering scope for catch-up. The riskreward profile remains favourable, and we continue to advocate broad-based accumulation. All in, we maintainOVERWEIGHTcall and reiterateBUYratings on Affin, AMMB, CIMB, Maybank, Public, and RHB. Last week, BNM published its 1H25 Financial Stability Review Report.We have compiled the extracted key highlights relevant to the banking sector as follows: Resilient banking system.The banking system continues to be sound and highly supportive of financial intermediation activities. This resilience is underpinned by healthy capital and liquidity buffers: (i) steady CET1 ratio of 14.7% (2024: 15.1%), (ii) robust LCR of 161% (2024: 161%), and (iii) NSFR of 116% (2024: 116%). Risks to financial stability (encompassing credit, market, liquidity, and contagion channels) remained well-contained in 1H25. Further illustrating system strength, the banking system's LLC ratio (including regulatory reserves) has been stabilised at level of 130.4% vs 2024: 129.6% and 2015-19 average: 114%. HH debt level remained safe.The overall quality of Household (HH) debt remains healthy. Total household (HH) debt-to-GDP inched up to 84.8% (2024: 84.1%), which was mainly driven by housing and car loans. Indications confirm HH borrowers debtservicing capacity remained healthy, where the median debt service ratio for outstanding and new lending was 33% and 41%, respectively (2024: 34% and 41%). The median debt-to-income ratio remained stable at 1.4x (2024: 1.4x). Furthermore, the aggregate financial asset-to-debt ratio stood firm at 2.1x (2024: 2.1x). Considering only liquid financial assets, the debt coverage is still a reassuring 1.3x (2024: 1.3x). In a positive trend, the share of HH loans labelled as having higher credit risk under Repayment Assistance (RA) has dropped by c.10bp to 1.7% (2024: 1.8%). Biz as usual.For businesses (Biz), the interest coverage ratio slid to 6.2x (2024: 6.5x), while the cash-to-short-term debt ratio remained stable at 1.5x (2024: 1.5x). Meanwhile, the debt-to-equity (D/E) ratio improved by 1ppt to 21.4% (2024: 22.4%). We believe this may reflect a cautious deleveraging trend across the board, likely driven by global trade uncertainties and a wait-and-see approach. Separately, the share of firms-at-risk declined to 24.4% (2024: 25.7%), remaining below the 3Q20 peak of 30.3%, although still elevated relative to the pre-pandemic average of 20.3% (20152019). Similarly, the share of Stage 2 Biz loans was unchanged at 11.9%, while the share of SME borrowings under RA improved to 4.1% of total SME loans (Dec 2024: 4.4%). MaintainOVERWEIGHT.We continue to favour the banking sector, underpinned by attractive valuations (currently trading at -2SD to its pre-Covid 5-year mean P/B, despite delivering comparable ROE), and a compelling dividend yield of c.5%. KLFINs YTD underperformance relative to the KLCI presents a catch-up opportunity, while its large-cap profile positions it well for foreign inflows amid EM rotation. Against this backdrop, we see a favourable risk-reward profile and recommend broad-based accumulation. We maintain BUY calls on: Affin (TP: RM3.00), AMMB (TP: RM6.20), CIMB (TP: RM8.80), Maybank (TP: RM10.70), Public (TP: RM5.10), and RHB (TP: RM7.70). Source:Hong Leong Investment Bank Research- 21 Oct 2025

Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM) held an engagement session last Friday with the release of the Financial Stability Review (FSR) 1H 2025. BNMs 1H 2025 FSR indicates that domestic financial stability is expected to remain broadly intact. This outlook is supported by sustained domestic demand, an uptick in investment activity, steady income growth, and a deep and liquid financial market. Financial institutions continue to maintain ample loss-absorbing buffers, positioning them well to support economic activity even under stress scenarios. Despite persistent volatility and uncertainty in global markets, Malaysian banks sustained strong capitalisation levels while delivering healthy profitability. The FSR noted that local financial markets continued to function in an orderly manner, anchored by strong performance in the bond market. Corporate bond fundraising remained active, with total issuance between March and August 2025 rising to RM79bn from RM64.1bn in the same period last year. Government securities also saw firm demand, with the average bid-to-cover ratio improving to 2.7x, higher than the 2.3x average recorded between 2022 and 2024. Additionally, higher activity levels in the FX and money markets during 1H 2025 further reflect the depth and resilience of domestic market liquidity. Business Sector Holding Firm The business sector continues to hold up well, supported by steady domestic demand and ongoing investment activity, despite external headwinds and rising cost pressures. Malaysias diversified export exposure remains a key buffer against fluctuations in global demand. According to the FSR, corporate debt servicing ability remains sound, with a median interest coverage ratio of 6.2x and operating margins averaging 7.3%, indicating healthy profitability. That said, sectors more sensitive to cost pressures, such as hotels and restaurants, are showing relatively higher levels of loans at risk. BNM has highlighted these pockets but views them as contained and under close supervision. No new signs of systemic stress have emerged within the SME segment. However, SMEs with existing financial vulnerabilities continue to face repayment pressures, reflected in a marginal uptick in Stage 2 and impaired loan ratios to 3.6% from 3.5% in December 2024. Despite this, access to repayment assistance remains readily available for distressed SMEs. Joint efforts by financial institutions and AKPK, combined with SMEs own cost rationalisation measures and use of internal cash buffers, have helped sustain repayment capacity. Encouragingly, SME loans under repayment assistance improved to 4.1% of total SME loans, down from 4.4% in December 2024, while newly restructured and rescheduled SME loans declined to 0.3% of total outstanding SME loans compared to 0.5% previously. Household Debt Stability Household debt servicing capacity remains healthy, supported by sustained income growth on the back of a firm labour market and generally prudent borrowing behaviour. There are limited signs of broad-based stress among households, with indicators such as credit card revolving balances and missed repayment incidences among credit card revolvers holding steady. Key metrics, including household impairment ratios, debt service ratios, and the proportion of household loans under repayment assistance, show no signs of deterioration, reinforcing the view that household balance sheets remain resilient. That said, BNM continues to monitor the rapid expansion of Buy Now Pay Later (BNPL) schemes. BNPL transaction volume and value have risen 22.4% and 31.0% YTD to RM102.6bn and RM9.3bn, respectively. Outstanding BNPL debt has increased by 36% YTD to RM3.8bn, but its scale remains relatively small at just 0.2% of total household debt. Importantly, overdue BNPL accounts stand at 3.2%, lower than the 6% recorded during the segments initial surge in March 2023 and notably below delinquency trends in credit cards and personal financing. With the Consumer Credit Commission set to regulate BNPL providers, oversight of credit conduct risk in this segment is expected to strengthen further. Loan Growth and Asset Quality Overall asset quality remains healthy and stronger than pre-pandemic levels. Gross impaired loan (GIL) ratios for businesses and households are stable at 3.1% and 1.1%, respectively. Most pandemic-era management overlays have already been unwound, with new overlays now reflecting institution-specific macro views rather than systemic stress. In 1H2025, credit deterioration was largely isolated to businesses with pre-existing financial weaknesses rather than those directly impacted by trade tariffs. Looking ahead, the operating environment for businesses is expected to stay stable. Sustained domestic and regional demand, coupled with easing global commodity prices, should help alleviate cost pressures. Banks continue to engage clients proactively and maintain a constructive risk appetite, indicating no major tightening in credit stance. Financing conditions remain accommodative, supporting credit expansion across both household and business segments. Total loans grew 4.5% YoY in 1H2025, while household loan growth held steady at 5.9% YoY. Given the resilient financial system and healthy credit appetite, we maintain our sector loan growth assumption of 5.7% for 2025. While deposit growth is expected to trail loan growth, system liquidity remains ample, backed by strong Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) and Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR) levels. The increasing use of bond issuance as a funding avenue is seen as credit positive, offering more stable and longer-term funding structures. Notably, bond funding is not necessarily costlier than certain fixed deposit tranches and enhances funding diversification. A potential headwind is temporary margin compression following the July 2025 OPR cut. Lending rates are likely to adjust downwards more quickly than funding costs, especially with a high proportion of floating-rate loans in the system. In contrast, the presence of longer-term fixed deposit books may delay the repricing of funding costs, resulting in a timing mismatch. As such, some downward pressure on NIM is expected before stabilisation in subsequent quarters. Overall Banking Sector Strength The Malaysian banking sector remains fundamentally robust, supported by strong capitalisation, liquidity, and funding positions, with a total capital ratio of 18.2%, Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) of 160.5%, and Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR) of 115.7%, all comfortably above regulatory requirements. Operationally, banks continued to deliver reliable and efficient financial services, while payment and settlement systems remained stable and secure. Notably, the incidence of technological failures declined by 38%, and severe disruptions to online banking services were significantly reduced. Operational risk losses remained minimal, representing just 0.04% of total banking system capital. To further strengthen operational resilience, banks and regulators are focusing on enhancing fraud prevention mechanisms, tightening API security governance, and improving consumer protectionparticularly for cross-border payment transactions. Looking ahead into 2026, BNM highlighted several external headwinds that could affect sentiment and financial conditions. These include persistent global trade uncertainties, uneven growth trajectories, and shifts in global monetary policy, which may introduce volatility into domestic financial markets. Additionally, ongoing trade disruptions and elevated input costs could continue to exert pressure on vulnerable business segments and lower-income households, although current buffers appear adequate to absorb potential stresses. Overweight Stance Maintained We maintain ourOVERWEIGHTstance on the banking sector. Our top recommendations for the sector includePublic BankandHong Leong Bank. However, we also haveBUYrecommendations forMaybank,CIMB,RHB Bank,Affin BankandAlliance Bank. We adjustAMMBfrom buy toHOLDas the risk-reward potential has narrowed to 12%. Source:TA Research- 21 Oct 2025

Review & Outlook The local benchmark index is likely to continue consolidating with a downside bias in the near term, as technical momentum and trend indicators turn increasingly negative. Broader market sentiment remains subdued, with investors awaiting clearer catalysts before any meaningful recovery can materialize. Externally, investor sentiment is likely to stay cautious, with market participants holding back on major trading decisions ahead of key U.S. tech earnings reports and the outcome of China's Fourth Plenum this week, which could provide greater clarity on the economic trajectories of the world's two largest economies. As for the index, immediate support is maintained at the 61.8%FR (1,564) with stronger supports seen at the 50%FR (1,527) followed by the 38.2%FR (1,490). Immediate resistance continues to be the December 2024 high of 1,644, with the highs of 1,684, and 1,695 as tougher upside hurdles. On near-term stock picks, any further corrections in key index heavyweights and lower liners such as Maybank, Public Bank, MRCB, Pharmaniaga, SKP Resources, UEM Sunrise, VSI, and WCT Holdings should attract bargain hunters looking for recovery play. PETALING JAYA: Maybank has clarified that a social media post advertising female usher positions for a Maybank ladies golf tournament was fake and had no connection to the bank, its organisers, or official vendors. The post, which had gone viral in recent days, stated it was looking for usherettes for the 2025 Maybank Championship and listed specific racial preferences  Chinese, Malay, fair Indian. It also mentioned the event dates as Oct 30 to Nov 2 at the Kuala Lumpur Golf & Country Club (KLGCC)  the venue and dates of the 2025 Maybank Championship. In a statement, Maybank said it was aware of the post and emphasised that the individual or account behind it was not associated with Maybank, the Maybank Championship, or any of its authorised event partners. The post used discriminatory language and misused Maybanks name. It has since been removed, but we urge everyone to remain vigilant, the bank said. Maybank strongly condemns any form of discrimination and remains committed to upholding equity, inclusivity, and integrity in all aspects of our events and partnerships. Maybank also advised the public not to engage with or share personal information through unverified sources, stressing that all legitimate job or volunteer opportunities related to its events are announced only through official and verified channels. This years edition of the Maybank Championship offers total prize money of US$3 million (RM12.7 million), the largest in Asia for a Ladies Professional Golf Association (LPGA) event. Among those expected to tee up at the event are Thailands world No 1 Jeeno Thitikul, and New Zealands Lydia Ko, the reigning Olympic gold medallist and a World Golf Hall of Famer.

KUALA LUMPUR (Oct 2): Malayan Banking Bhd (KL:MAYBANK) has received an upgrade in its MSCI ESG rating from AA to AAA. In a statement Thursday, Maybank said that the upgrade was driven by improvements in key areas of its environmental, social, and governance (ESG) performance, particularly within the environmental and social pillars. Maybanks environmental pillar score rose to 9.5 out of 10, anchored by the banks efforts in financing environmental impact, supported by its relatively low to moderate exposure in financing activities to environmentally-intensive sectors and the establishment of a dedicated team overseeing ESG risks in financing activities. The improvement in the social pillar score was driven by stronger performance in human capital development, access to finance, and consumer financial protection, it said. KUALA LUMPUR (Oct 1): Investors are advised to buy bank stocks and accumulate positions across major lenders, as analysts see the sectors upside outweighing risks. Hong Leong Investment Bank Research (HLIB Research) expects some net interest margin (NIM) pressure in the third quarter due to Julys overnight policy rate (OPR) cut. However, it noted that the sector is currently trading about two standard deviations below its pre-Covid five-year average price-to-book ratio, despite delivering comparable returns on equity. It added that the Bursa Malaysia Financial Services Index continues to trail the FBM KLCI, leaving room for upside. The sector is also supported by cheap valuations and attractive dividend yields of around 5%. We see a favourable risk-reward setup and recommend broad-based accumulation, HLIB Research said in a note on Wednesday. Its top picks are Affin Bank Bhd (KL:AFFIN), AMMB Holdings Bhd (KL:AMBANK), CIMB Group Holdings Bhd (KL:CIMB), Malayan Banking Bhd (KL:MAYBANK), Public Bank Bhd (KL:PBBANK) and RHB Bank Bhd (KL:RHBBANK). Meanwhile, Kenanga Research also remains bullish on the sector, citing resilient earnings and dividends. It said the July OPR cut could spur loan demand but may pressure deposits, possibly prompting banks to explore alternative funding sources. Even so, it expects seasonal year-end campaigns to support deposit growth. In spite of added caution over US tariffs, return-on-equity (ROE) expectations remain stable with slight upside, which should anchor sentiment on the sector in the near term, Kenanga said. CIMB Research, on the other hand, expects loan growth to pick up in 4Q2025, though it cautioned that the impact of Julys OPR cut on credit demand remains unclear. It flagged upside risks such as lower credit costs, improved margins, and stronger fee income, while noting that downside risks include higher funding costs and weaker asset quality. CIMB maintains a neutral stance on the sector. Source: TheEdge - 2 Oct 2025

System loan and deposit growth remained stable, up 5.4% and 3.9% YoY respectively.Loan applications rebounded but banks remain selective in their approval processes. Asset quality stays sound for now and the elevated LLC level of c.90% provides good buffers. Looking ahead, we anticipate NIM compression in 3Q25, underpinned by the 25bp OPR cut in July. Nevertheless, we reiterate our positive stance on the banking sector, supported by undemanding valuations and an attractive dividend yield of c.5%. Additionally, KLFIN continues to lag the KLCI YTD, offering scope for catch-up. In our view, the sectors risk-reward profile remains compelling, and we advocate a broadbased accumulation strategy. MaintainOVERWEIGHTcall and reiterateBUYratings on Affin, AMMB, CIMB, Maybank, Public, and RHB. Steady system loans growth for Aug at 5.4% YoY (Jul: 5.4%), driven by robust performance in the Biz segment (5.2% YoY; Jul: 4.9%) and stable HH loans expansion (5.7% YoY; Jul: 5.8%).The slight uptick in Biz loans was largely driven by increased working capital requirements among non-SMEs. Overall, system loans growth remains comfortably within our FY25 forecast range of 5.05.5%. Loan applications rebounded to 2.2% YoY in Aug (Jul: -1.1%), led by a resilient HH segment (3.3% YoY; Jul: 0.0%), while Biz applications remained flat (0.9% YoY; Jul: -2.4%).Despite this, loan approvals edged down by 0.2% YoY (Jul: -5.1%), weighed by continued weakness in Biz approvals (-2.4% YoY; Jul: -8.3%). In contrast, HH approvals improved to 2.3% YoY (Jul: -1.8%), reflecting better lending credit by banks in this area. Resilient deposit dynamics contribute to a firm deposit growth of 3.8% YoY (Jul: 3.7%), supported by robust foreign savings inflows (17.0% YoY; Jul: 13.7%), alongside steady gains in CASA (6.1% YoY; Jul: 6.6%).The systems loan-todeposit ratio was flat MoM in Aug-25 at c.89%. Asset quality remains healthy for now, with Aug-25 GIL ratio holding steady at 1.43% (-1bp MoM).Both Biz and HH GILs were unchanged sequentially at 1.06% and 2.00%, respectively. Nonetheless, we maintain a constructive view on asset quality, given the persistently high LLC level of c.90% since the onset of Covid-19. KUALA LUMPUR (Sept 29): Vantris Energy Bhd, (KL:VANTNRG) formerly Sapura Energy Bhd, has named Adnan Zainol Abidin as its new chairman, effective Oct 1. Adnan, who previously served as chief operating officer at Petroliam Nasional Bhd, brings over four decades of oil and gas experience. He was also executive vice-president and chief executive officer of Petronas' gas business, Petronas Gas Bhd (KL:PETGAS). He is succeeding Shahin Farouque Jammal Ahmad, who will be relinquishing his position on the same day. Shahin is currently group head of strategic investments at Permodalan Nasional Bhd. At the same time, Vantris Energy said Datuk Shahriman Shamsuddin  who resigned from the board in June  has ceased to be a substantial shareholder following the completion of the groups regularisation plan to exit its Practice Note 17 (PN17) status, which included a capital reduction and debt restructuring to cut its borrowings from RM10.8 billion to RM5.6 billion. Shahriman's stake was diluted to 0.02% after the issuance of 1.36 billion settlement shares on Sept 26. Shahriman and his brother, former president and group CEO Tan Sri Shahril Shamsuddin, had previously held an indirect 11.25% interest via Brothers Capital Sdn Bhd. Completion of the regularisation plan also saw the emergence of three major banks as the group's substantial shareholders, namely Malayan Banking Bhd (KL:MAYBANK) with a 20.27% stake, CIMB Group Holdings Bhd (KL:CIMB) with 12.13%, and RHB Bank Bhd (KL:RHBBANK) with 7.21%. Shares of Vantris Energy closed 3.5 sen or 6.6% higher at 56.5 sen on Monday, giving the group a market capitalisation of RM1.29 billion. Source: TheEdge - 30 Sep 2025

KUALA LUMPUR (Sept 29): The restructuring of Vantris Energy Bhd (KL:VANTNRG), formerly known as Sapura Energy Bhd, could add as much as 2.3% to its creditor banks earnings this year, according to MBSB Research. In a note on Monday, the research house said creditor banks including Malayan Banking Bhd (KL:MAYBANK), CIMB Group Holdings Bhd (KL:CIMB) and RHB Bank Bhd (KL:RHBBANK), which emerged as major shareholders under the scheme, are expected to recognise earnings contributions from settlement shares issued at 80 sen apiece. At Vantris current market price of 53 sen, the proceeds translate into an uplift of between 1.5% and 2.3% of net profit this year, with Maybank likely to see the largest absolute impact. Most banks were already guiding for stronger recoveries in 2H2025 (second half of 2025)  hence these proceeds are likely embedded in most banks guidance. Regardless, this is a step in a positive direction, MBSB Research said. Despite banks having been guiding for larger recoveries in 2H2025, the full writebacks from the oil-and-gas firms proceeds are unlikely, said MBSB Research. A clear-cut writeback based on the value of the proceeds is unlikely, as most banks are likely to retain a fair chunk of their provisions amid economic volatility, it noted. Bright spots for dividends The banking outlook, meanwhile, remains resilient with extremely attractive dividend yields, MBSB Research flagged, as it kept its positive stance on the sector. There are hints of higher dividend payouts at years end, MBSB Research said, adding that wealth management, bancassurance and debt capital markets are expected to drive non-interest income (NOII) growth in 2025. Most lenders also maintain elevated liquidity coverage ratios, giving them room to offset net interest margin (NIM) compression, following the recent overnight-policy-rate (OPR) cut. MBSB Research named RHB Bank and Hong Leong Bank Bhd (KL:HLBANK) as its top picks, due to their high dividend yields, resilient asset quality, and strong NOII growth. Source: TheEdge - 30 Sep 2025

(RTT) UK Mortgage Approvals Decline; Consumer Credit Remains Unchanged UK mortgage approvals declined in August ahead of the autumn budget and consumer credit remained flat, official data showed Monday. The number of mortgage approvals, which is an indicator of future borrowing, fell by 500 to 64,700 in August, the Bank of England reported. This was also below the expected level of 65,000. The actual interest paid on newly drawn mortgages declined for the sixth month in a row. The 'effective rate' hit 4.26% in August compared to 4.28% in July. The BoE had reduced its benchmark interest rate five times since August 2024. The bank rate reached 4.00%, the lowest since early 2023. Secured lending dropped by GBP0.2bn to GBP4.3bn in August after falling by GBP0.9bn in July, data showed today. Nonetheless, the annual growth rate rose to 3.0% from 2.9%. Net borrowing of consumer credit by individuals remained flat at GBP1.7bn in August. On a yearly basis, consumer credit logged an annual growth of 7.1%, slightly faster than July's 7.0% rise. UK businesses borrowed GBP3.2bn of loans from banks and building societies, following net borrowing of GBP5.3bn in July. (RTT) UK's Reeves Leaves Door Open for More Tax Rises at Budget British finance minister Rachel Reeves said on Monday the world had changed since she last pledged not to raise taxes, paving the way for a November budget in which she acknowledged that tough choices needed to be made to keep to her fiscal rules. Ahead of a speech at the governing Labour Party's conference on Monday, she told the BBC that pledges not to raise income or VAT sales taxes stood, but hinted that some tax increases would be necessary at the next fiscal event on November 26. Asked whether she could repeat a statement she made to businesses last November that she was not coming back with more borrowing or more taxes, Reeves said: "I think everyone can see the world has changed, and we're not immune to that change." She cited US President Donald Trump's tariffs, global conflicts and a possible productivity downgrade by the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) as impacting the outlook. " Reeves also said she wanted the OBR to shift to providing only one set of budget forecasts a year, rather than a mid-year update as well  which earlier this year required government action to keep government budget goals on track. (Reuters) Source:TA Research- 30 Sept 2025

KUALA LUMPUR (Sept 29): Malayan Banking Bhd (KL:MAYBANK) has moved its internal audit chief Mohamad Yasin Abdullah to head its Islamic banking business effective Oct 1. Mohamad Yasin will officially succeed the position of CEO at Maybank Islamic Bhd from Datuk Muzaffar Hisham, who stepped down in April to helm Bank Pembangunan Malaysia Bhd, according to a statement on Monday. Yasin will be tasked with leading the Islamic banking franchise, overseeing business strategies, product development and innovation while ensuring risk management and Shariah compliance, the company said. He will report directly to Maybank president Datuk Khairussaleh Ramli and serve as a member of the executive committee at the countrys largest bank by assets. Yasins current role as Maybanks group chief audit executive will be taken over by Malique Firdauz Ahmad Sidique, Maybank said. Malique Firdauz, 47, has been overseeing the finances of Maybank Islamic before the latest management reshuffling. In his role, he will report functionally to the audit committee of the board and administratively to the president of Maybank. The appointments demonstrate the effectiveness of Maybanks internal succession planning and talent management practices, said Khairussaleh in the statement. This will be vital in ensuring that we are well-positioned to steer Maybank forward as we unlock its full potential in the next phase of our strategic growth as well as continue delivering exceptional value to our customers and stakeholders, he added. Source: TheEdge - 30 Sep 2025

Positive Expectations to Buffer Trumps Tariffs Positive Expectations to Buffer Trumps Tariffs The local blue-chip benchmark FTSE Bursa Malaysia Kuala Lumpur Composite Index (FBM KLCI) remained largely rangebound over the week, weighed down by a weak market undertone and a lack of positive catalysts to drive buying momentum. Comments by U.S. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell expressing caution over further monetary easing also dented local market sentiment. However, the index later staged a mild rebound spurred by bargain hunting activity in selected blue-chip heavyweights to end the week above the1600 psychological level. On a week-to-week basis, the FBM KLCI rose 10.82 points, or 0.68 % to 1,609.05, as gains in 99 Speed Mart (14sen), Sime Darby (11sen), Petronas Chemicals (15sen), MRDIY (5sen) and Sunway (17sen) offset losses in Axiata (-11sen), CelcomDigi (-13sen) and MISC (-23sen). Average daily traded volume last week increased to 3.46 billion shares versus the 3.1 billion shares the previous week, while average daily traded value fell to RM2.67 billion against the RM3.28 billion average the previous week. President Trumps announcement of sweeping new tariffs, effective 1 October 2025, has reignited trade concerns.The measures include a 100% tariff on imported branded pharmaceuticals, a 50% tariff on kitchen and bathroom cabinets, a 30% tariff on upholstered furniture, and a 25% tariff on imported semi-trucks. From a macro perspective, these tariffs are likely to raise costs for U.S. consumers already grappling with inflation, while also disrupting supply chains in industries where Southeast Asian exporters dominate. Malaysian players may need to accelerate diversification into ASEAN, Middle Eastern, or African markets to mitigate U.S. concentration risk. For Malaysian exporters, the most immediate pressure falls onPoh Huat Resources (Sell, TP: RM0.96), which generates more than 90 % of its revenue from furniture exports to the United States. With upholstered furniture directly targeted, the company faces significant margin compression unless it can pass on costs or diversify markets. Historically, Poh Huats U.S. sales have been price-sensitive, suggesting limited ability to absorb such a steep tariff. Meanwhile, media reports said that the US is considering granting tariff exemptions to Malaysia on selected items, including furniture. According to Malaysias chief negotiator, Mastura Ahmad Mustafa, a decision could be finalised as early as next month. (See our company update today Trump Slaps 30% Tariff on Upholstered Furniture Imports). On the pharmaceutical side,Duopharma Biotech (Buy, TP: RM1.70)produces generic drugs and does not export to the US. Since the 100 % tariff applies to branded and patented drugs, there is no impact on Duopharma. However, sentiment toward the broader sector could soften if global supply chains are disrupted or if U.S. buyers shift procurement strategies. The local benchmark index is expected to take the latest tariff and inflation headlines in stride, supported by renewed foreign buying interest following the U.S. Federal Reserves recent monetary easing.That momentum is likely to continue after last Fridays U.S. inflation release, which came broadly in line with expectations and reassured markets that the Fed remains on track for further cuts this year. The August personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index rose 0.3% MoM, lifting the annual headline rate to 2.7% from 2.6% in July, while the core PCE index held steady at 2.9% after a 0.2 % monthly gain. These readings, consistent with consensus forecasts, are unlikely to alter the Feds easing path at its October meeting. Markets continue to price in two additional rate cuts before year-end, despite Chair Jerome Powells cautious tone on the pace of further easing. Core PCE inflation has drifted upward through the summer, rising from 2.8% in June to 2.9% in July and remaining at that level in August. On a monthly basis, the index climbed 0.3% in July before moderating to 0.2% in August. This pattern underscores sticky underlying price pressures above the Feds 2% target, reflecting both tariff pass-through and persistent service-sector costs. Policymakers will be watching closely in the fourth quarter to see if these pressures ease as import duties stabilize and demand cools. Nonetheless, under its flexible average-inflation targeting framework, the Fed is expected to tolerate above-target readings temporarily, given the transitory nature of tariff-driven inflation and the need to prioritize its employment mandate.In its September Summary of Economic Projections, the Feds median forecast placed headline PCE at 3.0 % and core PCE at 3.1 % for 2025, before gradually converging toward 2.6 % and 2.1 % in 2026 and 2027, respectively. The median unemployment rate is projected at 4.5 % in 2025, easing to 4.3 % by 2027. Against this backdrop, the upcoming September employment report will be pivotal, with markets focused on nonfarm payrolls, unemployment, and wage growth. In Asia, sentiment could be buoyed by Chinas industrial profit rebound.Profits of major industrial firms surged 20.4 % YoY in August, reversing a 1.5 % decline in July. For the JanuaryAugust period, profits rose 0.9 %, ending a prolonged contraction. The turnaround reflects Beijings efforts to curb excess capacity and rein in cutthroat competition, particularly in autos, solar, and heavy industry. Further improvement in Septembers PMI readings this week would add credibility to the recovery narrative. Locally, attention is turning to the tabling of Budget 2026 on 10 October.The FBMKLCI has already rebounded more than 200 points from its low of 1,386 earlier this year, and historical patterns suggest a tendency for pre-budget optimism. Since the Asian Financial Crisis, the two weeks before the budget have delivered gains in 65.5 % of years, with an average return of 1.1 %. Post-budget performance, however, has been inconsistent. While positive years can see strong follow-throughaverage gains of 5.3 % two months after and 9.8 % three months afterthe downside years have been equally sharp, with average losses of around 7.3 to 7.4 %. On balance, average returns post-budget are slightly negative in the near term, before turning modestly positive at the three-month mark. This history suggests that the budget itself is not a reliable catalyst for sustained rallies. Short-term optimism often gives way to volatility as investors digest fiscal measures, sectoral allocations, and broader macro conditions. For positioning, fundamentals and sector-specific implications matter more than assuming a uniform budget rally. Construction and utilities remain the most consistent beneficiaries of fiscal policy, supported by infrastructure spending and the energy transition agenda. Industrials tied to high-value manufacturing and exports also stand to gain from structural policy support. By contrast, energy, healthcare, and technology remain volatile, swinging with global cycles and sectorspecific shocks. Property and REITs continue to deliver mixed results, often lagging unless directly supported by housing incentives or infrastructure-driven demand, while energy remains more closely tied to global oil price movements than to domestic policy. PETALING JAYA: Creditor banks including Malayan Banking Bhd (Maybank), CIMB Group Holdings Bhd and RHB Bank Bhd will get a boost to earnings from the restructuring of Vantris Energy Bhd, formerly known as Sapura Energy Bhd. The implementation of the oil and gas service providers regularisation plan to exit its Practice Note 17 (PN17) status resulted in Maybank, CIMB, and RHB emerging as major shareholders of the rebranded entity. In a note today, MBSB Research said the restructuring of Vantris could add as much as 2.3% to its creditor banks earnings this year. It said the creditor banks are expected to recognise earnings contributions from settlement shares issued at 80 sen apiece. Based on the current market price of 53 sen per share, the proceeds translate into an uplift of between 1.5% and 2.3% of net profit this year, it noted. The research house also said Maybank was likely to see the largest absolute impact. Malaysias largest bank ended up with a 20.27% stake in Vantris, post restructuring. In a bourse filing last Friday, Maybank said Vantris had issued 462.45 million new shares and 498.48 million redeemable convertible unsecured Islamic debt securities (RCUIDS) to the bank and its unit Maybank Islamic Bhd. This formed part of the settlement of liabilities owed by Sapura TMC Sdn Bhd, a wholly owned subsidiary of Vantris. In a separate filing, CIMB disclosed its subsidiaries also received new securities. CIMB Bank Bhd obtained 208.93 million shares and 225.21 million RCUIDS, while CIMB Islamic Bank Bhd received 67.73 million shares and 73.01 million RCUIDS. Together, they hold a combined 12.13% stake in Vantris. Meanwhile, RHB said its subsidiary RHB Islamic Bank received 164.56 million shares and 177.39 million RCUIDS, giving the banking group a 7.21% indirect stake in the company. MBSB noted that other listed creditors, particularly AMMB Holdings Bhd (AMMB), did not issue announcements, likely being short of the 5% shareholding disclosure threshold. Exiting PN17 Vantris had secured approval from the stock exchange regulator in June for its regularisation plan to exit its PN17 classification, which it fell under in 2022 due to financial distress. Under the plan, the company will perform a 99.99% capital reduction to offset accumulated losses, along with a 20-to-one share consolidation to slash the number of shares in circulation. The debt restructuring will reduce its borrowings from RM10.8 billion to about RM5.6 billion, cutting annual interest expenses by more than RM500 million, or approximately 60%. The plan also involves a RM1.1 billion capital injection from the ministry of finance, via Malaysia Development Holding Sdn Bhd (MDH), through redeemable convertible loan stocks (RCLS). Proceeds from this subscription have been earmarked to settle outstanding payments to Malaysian oil and gas vendors. Upon full conversion of the RCLS, MDH could emerge as Vantris single largest shareholder with over a 33% stake, overtaking Permodalan Nasional Bhd, whose stake may be diluted to just above 5%. Vantris closed 6.6% or 3.5 sen higher at 56 sen, valuing the group at RM1.29 billion.

PETALING JAYA: Malayan Banking Bhd (Maybank) has appointed Yasin Abdullah as group CEO of Islamic banking and CEO of Maybank Islamic Bhd, effective Oct 1. In a statement today, Maybank said Yasin will be responsible for leading the groups Islamic banking franchise and spearheading Maybanks vision of becoming a global leader in Islamic finance, Bernama reported. He will oversee all aspects of Maybank groups Islamic finance business, including business strategies, product development and innovation, while ensuring risk management and shariah-compliant management are of gold standards. He will report to Maybanks president and GCEO, and be a member of the group executive committee, it said. Yasin, 52, is currently Maybanks group chief audit executive (GCAE), where he spearheaded the Maybank Group Audit Strategic Plan 2023-2025 to elevate the audit function through global best practices and enhance assurance across the group. Meanwhile, Maybank has also appointed Malique Firdauz Ahmad Sidique, 47, as its new GCAE, effective Oct 1, taking over from Yasin. Maybank president and GCEO Khairussaleh Ramli said that these appointments continued to demonstrate the effectiveness of Maybanks internal succession planning.
