Loan growth has started to lose steam, with softer numbers from March 2025 compared to the strong momentum in late 2024. The slowdown is largely due to rising global uncertainties and a more cautious tone in both business and consumer sentiment. Many corporates have shifted to a wait-and-see approach, while consumer loan growth has also eased slightly from the 6.5% YoY seen in April 2024. Considering these developments, our overall loan growth forecast for 2025 has been revised down from 6.4% to 5.7% YoY. Net interest margins (NIM) are expected to remain stable, though mild compression is possible depending on policy rate movements, funding dynamics, and competition. While some banks are bracing for a potential OPR cut, most have not yet factored it into their official guidance. If a cut does happen, we estimate that sector earnings could dip by around 1.8%. However, Bank Negara Malaysias (BNM) recent move to lower the Statutory Reserve Requirement (SRR) from 2% to 1% should help offset the impact, injecting liquidity and potentially boosting the sectors earnings by about 1.9%. Non-interest income (non-NII) will likely play a bigger role in supporting revenue, driven by trading and investment income, as well as steady growth in fee-based segments like bancassurance and wealth management. Non-NII is becoming more strategic as banks invest in partnerships and digital tools to diversify their revenue streams and enhance returns. The stability in loan demand also provides a solid base for maintaining fee income. Banks remain focused on managing costs tightly while continuing to invest in digital transformation. Although IT spending is expected to rise as part of long-term strategic priorities, banks are balancing this with broader cost control initiatives. As a result, the sectors cost-to-income ratio is projected to stay at around 46% in 2025, with steady income growth providing a cushion against rising expenses. Asset quality remains resilient, supported by prudent risk management despite pockets of sector-specific stress. The system-wide gross impaired loans ratio has held steady at around 1.43%, and credit costs are expected to decline further. While banks have started releasing some management overlays, they continue to hold substantial provision buffers as a safeguard. With solid fundamentals, strong capital positions, and attractive valuations, the KL Finance Index (KLFI) continues to outperform the FBM KLCI. We maintain our OVERWEIGHT stance on the sector, given its resilience, earnings visibility, and capacity to support credit flows and economic stability. Our stock picks for the sector are CIMB Group Holdings Berhad (TP: RM8.86, ), Hong Leong Bank Berhad (TP: RM23.59, ), and Public Bank Berhad (TP: RM5.15, ). Loans and Advances Business Loans Loan Growth Momentum Loses Steam Amid Global Uncertainty Loan growth has started to ease, coming in below expectations and marking a break from the stronger momentum seen in the second half of 2024. To recap, loan activity had been picking up steadily during that period, with a notable rebound in business loans as confidence and demand improved. However, this momentum did not to carry through into March and the 2Q of 2025, dampened by rising global uncertainty following Donald Trump's return to the U.S. presidency and renewed volatility stemming from trade and tariff tensions. Since March 2025, business sentiment has turned cautious, with many companies adopting a "wait-and-see" approach. This shift has begun to show in Bank Negara Malaysia's (BNM) monthly data. According to BNM, business loan growth moderated to 4.1% YoY in April 2025, down from a stronger 5.4% YoY expansion in April 2024. Loans for working capital also grew at a slower pace of 4.1% YoY (April 2024: 5.3%), reflecting softer overall business activity. On a sector level, loans to the manufacturing sector  which account for roughly 15% of total loans  remained muted, registering a 4.4% YoY growth in April 2025. This aligns with Malaysia's recent Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) readings, which suggest that demand conditions in the manufacturing sector remained lacklustre during the month. Both production and new orders continued to weaken, weighed down by subdued domestic and external demand. Manufacturers reported ongoing declines in new business inflows, and with external markets still showing limited signs of recovery, production volumes may face further pressure. In response, many firms are expected to scale back purchasing activity and continue drawing down on existing raw materials and finished goods inventory to manage costs more effectively Construction Loan Growth Weakens, But Structural Tailwinds Remain The construction sector, which we had anticipated to perform more strongly, has recorded declining loan growth for five consecutive months. The latest figures from April 2025 show a 7.6% YoY contraction. Construction loans comprise around 11% of total business loans, making this a notable drag on overall lending momentum. That said, not all segments are slowing. Earlier concerns over the US restrictions on AI chip exports  which raised fears of a broader tech slowdown and a potential pullback in data centre investments appear overstated as construction activity related to data centres remains resilient. Developers continue to tap into rising demand driven by the artificial intelligence, cloud computing, and Internet of Things (IoT) sectors, with several new data centre projects already in the pipeline. Beyond the tech-driven demand, construction activity is also expected to be supported by government-led initiatives, particularly flood mitigation. At the same time, rising demand for housing and industrial properties, along with strategic projects such as the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ), the Special Financial Zone (SFZ) in Forest City, and urbanisation efforts in areas like Seberang Perai and Batu Kawan, are contributing to a solid project pipeline. These developments are expected to underpin the medium-term growth of the construction and non-residential property sectors. Pockets of Business Loan Growth Remain, But Broader Momentum Softens Despite the recent moderation in overall business loan growth, we continue to see selective areas of opportunity. Notably, loans in the Electricity, Gas, Steam & Air Conditioning Supply sector surged by a strong 24.5% YoY in April 2025. This sharp increase aligns with Malaysias push toward clean energy under the National Energy Transition Roadmap (NETR), which estimates that up to RM1.85 trillion in financing will be required to support the countrys energy transition efforts by 2050. With this backdrop, we believe the outlook remains positive for financing opportunities in renewable power generation, green mobility, and emerging technologies such as hydrogen and carbon capture, utilisation and storage (CCUS). On the other hand, several industries continued to report weaker lending activity. Loans to the Education, Health & Others and Agriculture, Forestry & Fishing sectors declined by 7.1% and 8.8% YoY, respectively, while Mining & Quarrying fell by 3.8% YoY in April. Most other sectors, however, still posted positive YoY growth. Leading the gains were Information & Communication (13.0% YoY), Water Supply, Sewerage & Waste Management (10.3% YoY), Finance,Insurance& Business Activities (10.0% YoY), followed by Accommodation & Food Services (7.6% YoY), Transportation & Storage (7.6% YoY), Manufacturing (4.4% YoY), and Wholesale & Retail Trade (3.4% YoY). Still, the YTD business loan growth remains below expectations, prompting us to revise our full-year business loan growth forecast for 2025, down from 5.5% to 4.9%. Adding to the cautious outlook, the latest RAM Business Confidence Index (BCI) for 1Q 2025 showed a notable deterioration in business sentiment. The index fell to 41.1 from 54.5 in the previous quarter  slipping back into pessimistic territory after four straight quarters of optimism. According to RAM, the weaker outlook reflects growing concerns over escalating global risks, particularly disruptions to global trade flows. These are expected to directly hit export sales and indirectly weigh on domestic business activity. Consumer Loans Consumer Loan Growth Moderates Amid Rising Cost Pressures Consumer loans, which comprise nearly 60% of total loans, continued to expand in April 2025, increasing 5.8% YoY and 0.4% MoM. However, this marked a slight moderation compared to the 6.5% YoY growth recorded in April 2024. Residentialmortgages accounting for around 65% of total consumer loans  remained the key driver, although growth slowed to 6.5% YoY from 7.5% a year earlier. Meanwhile, loans for purchasing passenger cars rose 7.2% YoY, easing from the stronger 10.1% growth recorded last year. Credit card lending and personal loans also grew slower, up 7.9% YoY (April 2024: 8.9%) and 3.3% YoY (April 2024: 5.7%), respectively. Loans for the purchase of securities remained in contraction, declining 2.8% YoY  though this was a narrower drop compared to -10.3% in March. Despite relatively healthy growth, YTD consumer loan expansion is also trailing our earlier forecast of 6.7% for 2025. While Malaysias Consumer Confidence Index rose by 4% in January 2025  buoyed by improving sentiment around job security, future investment plans, and purchasing power  there are several headwinds. These include the rising cost of living due to subsidy rationalisation, as well as external pressures from ongoing trade tensions and geopolitical instability. These factors may prompt consumers to become more cautious in the second half of the year, potentially slowing borrowing activity. As a result, we revised our full-year consumer loan growth forecast lower to 6.3%. Key Consumer Loan Segments to Moderate, But Underlying Demand Remains Supportive We project residential mortgages to grow at a slightly softer pace of 6.9% YoY in 2025  down from our earlier forecast of 7.4% YoY. Nonetheless, the outlook remains broadly optimistic, supported by resilient domestic demand, a stable labour market, accommodative monetary policy, and ongoing government efforts to encourage homeownership. We continue to expect the property sector to see stronger momentum this year, with developers under our coverage projected to post around 4% growth in property sales. Hire purchase (HP) loans for passenger cars  accounting for roughly 17% of total consumer loans  are also expected to grow more modestly, at 7.3% YoY in 2025 vs 8.5% in 2024. This moderation is largely due to a high base effect following several years of strong growth in total industry volume (TIV). For 2025, we anticipate the automotive sector will begin to normalise, with vehicle sales forecasted to decline to 700,000 units (-14.3% YoY) as demand settles after prolonged elevated activity. The sizeable backlog of vehicle orders, which had previously supported growth, has mostly cleared. Despite that, HP loans will remain a key contributor to consumer loan growth. We continue to see sustained demand for certain automotive brands, supported by the rollout of new models, facelifts, and variant expansions  helping to partially offset the overall sector slowdown. Meanwhile, we now expect credit card loans to grow by 5.7% YoY and personal loans to rise by 4.0% YoY. Loan Growth Outlook Trimmed Amid Cautious Business Sentiment Bringing everything together, we have revised our overall loan growth forecast for 2025 downwards from 6.4% YoY to 5.7% YoY, reflecting more tempered expectations for both consumer and business loans. In line with this, we have also lowered our loan growth projections for all banks under coverage from 6.7% to 6.1%, as most financial institutions balance strategic caution and selective expansion. To recap the latest 1QCY25 results,Alliance Bankstood out with an impressive loan growth of 12% YoY  twice the industry pace  supported by strong momentum in SME lending (12% YoY), consumer loans (13% YoY) and targeted growth in economic corridors such as Penang (20% YoY) and Sarawak (17% YoY).Affin Bankalso delivered a solid performance, with gross loans rising 7.1% YoY, led by substantial gains in community banking (10%) and enterprise banking (7.5%). In contrast, other financial institutions have guided for more moderate growth in the months ahead. Banks cited a slowdown in loan demand, particularly in the commercial and corporate segments, as businesses delay investment decisions amid ongoing global tariff uncertainties. For instance,RHB Bankhas adopted a more cautious outlook, revising its 2025 loan growth guidance to 56% (from 67%), citing expectations of softer GDP growth. Similarly,AMMBandPublic Bankare both targeting mid-single-digit loan growth of around 56%, whileCIMBcontinues to emphasise disciplined, responsible asset expansion, particularly in the consumer and commercial segments. That said, select high-yield segments continue to see vigorous competitive activity.Public BankandMaybankflagged heightened competition in themortgageand SME space. ForAffin Bank, mortgage and HP loans remain key drivers, as the bank targets growth in national car financing to improve credit diversification.AMMBis also seeing traction in both HP and RSME (Retail SME) portfolios. Meanwhile,Hong Leong Bankaims to further grow its HP portfolio, with plans to have 10% of its auto loan origination fully digital by the second half of the year. Net Interest Margin NIM Is Likely to Stay Stable to Slightly Lower Amid Policy and Competitive Headwinds Net interest margins (NIMs) should remain broadly stable or face mild compression across the banking sector, shaped by evolving policy rate expectations, funding mix dynamics, and ongoing competitive pressures. While most banks have acknowledged the possibility of an Overnight Policy Rate (OPR) cut later this year, they have yet to factor it into their official NIM guidance. Nonetheless, we estimate that should an OPR cut materialise, it could shave approximately 1.8% off sector earnings. Beyond potential monetary easing, NIM compression also reflects lingering competitive pressure, particularly on the funding side. While the battle for deposits was significantly more intense last year, the situation has eased somewhat in recent months. According to BNMs April 2025 data, the industrys average deposit rates  which peaked in 1QCY2024  have since moderated and stabilised at around 2.69% for 12-month fixed deposits (FDs). The initial disruption caused by aggressive deposit campaigns from digital banks has also begun to normalise. For example,CIMBhas successfully lowered its campaign deposit rates by 1020 bps, supported by the groups strong capital and liquidity positions. While selective promotional FD campaigns are still in the market, they are now more contained, with most 12-month FD rates ranging between 3.80% and 4.10% as of May 2025. On the asset side, some banks are seeing rising price competition, particularly in business loans and the mortgage segment. This is reflected in the overall average lending rate, which has eased to 4.92% in April 2025, down from 4.97% in March and 5.36% a year ago. However, with deposit competition cooling, sector-wide NIMs have stabilised at around 2%. Liquidity and Capital Buffers Remain Healthy; SRR Cut Provides Added Relief The banking systems liquidity position remains solid, with the Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) rising slightly to around 156% in April 2025, up slightly from 152% a year ago. The loan-to-fund ratio also climbed to 83.3% (April 2024: 82.0%), reflecting sufficient liquidity buffers. On the capital front, banks continue to maintain strong positions, with a Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio of 14.3% and a Total Capital Ratio of 17.7%, well above regulatory minimums. Helping cushion the impact of any potential OPR cut and margin compression, BNMs decision last month to reduce the Statutory Reserve Requirement (SRR) from 2% to 1% has injected a welcome liquidity boost into the system. As highlighted in our earlier report, we view this move as a net positive for the sector. The estimated RM19bn liquidity release provides banks with added balance sheet flexibility  supporting loan growth and helping manage funding costs. Our projections suggest the improved liquidity could lift sector earnings by approximately 1.9%.AMMBandRHB Bank, for example, are set to receive around RM1.25bn and RM1.7bn, respectively, from this liquidity release. Banks Refining Asset Strategies; CASA Remains a Key Focus Despite Slowing Growth Amid margin pressures and evolving rate dynamics, banks are actively refining their asset and liability strategies to optimise funding costs and strengthen liquidity profiles. A key area of focus has been on improving the deposit mix  particularly in growing low-cost Current and Savings Account (CASA) deposits. Affin Bankstands out with a strong 36.2% YoY CASA growth in 1QCY25, surpassing its CASA mix target of 32%, largely supported by its push in community and enterprise banking.Alliance Bankalso posted a solid 13.1% YoY increase in demand deposits, bolstered by inflows from government accounts and stronger traction in emerging growth corridors such as Penang and Sarawak. Meanwhile,Maybankis actively rebalancing its deposit structure by reducing reliance on short-term wholesale money market deposits and shifting towards more stable, cost-efficient fixed deposits (FDs).AMMB, on the other hand, expects its margins to hold relatively steady even in the event of an OPR cut, thanks to ongoing adjustments in asset mix and strategic funding management  including a bond portfolio positioned to benefit from a declining rate environment. Still, on a system-wide level, CASA growth has shown signs of softening. As of April 2025, total CASA grew by 4.8% YoY (0.2% MoM), moderating from 6.1% growth a year ago. That said, there are still opportunities for banks to strengthen CASA traction. For instance,Affin Bankanticipates further CASA uplift from the Sarawak government payroll mandate and the rollout of its new mobile banking app, AffinAlwaysX.RHB Bankis also tapping into untapped segments through initiatives like its RHB MySiswa Debit Card-i programme  a collaboration with the Ministry of Higher Education  which has onboarded nearly 500,000 university students to date, driving incremental CASA growth while promoting financial literacy. Non-Interest Income Non-interest Income Growth to Moderate, But the Outlook Remains Positive For CY25 and CY26, we project non-interest income (non-NII) for the banks under our coverage to grow by 8.7% and 9.2%, respectively. While these are healthy rates, they mark a moderation from the strong 15.7% YoY jump in CY2024. To recap, the sharp rise in 2024 was largely underpinned by robust fee income, solid trading and investment gains, and favourable forex movements amid heightened market volatility. Looking ahead, we believe several drivers will continue to support non-NII growth in 2025 and beyond. Continued global market uncertainty  particularly around the pace of U.S. Fed rate cuts and currency movements  will likely sustain opportunities for trading and investment income. On the fee income side, which rose by 8.9% YoY in 2024, we expect a degree of stability underpinned by steady loan demand and growing momentum in the Bancassurance and Wealth Management segments. Encouragingly, 1QCY25 results already show positive trends:Hong Leong Bankled the pack with a 10.1% YoY rise in fee income, followed byCIMB(6.7%) andAlliance Bank(5.1%). Conversely, capital market activities have taken a step back. Although the prevailing stable interest rate environment offers a supportive backdrop for mergers, acquisitions, and other corporate exercises, heightened market volatility has led some companies to delay their IPO plans or revise offer prices downward. These decisions reflect growing concerns over weaker investor sentiment, reduced valuations, and a cautious market outlook. Nonetheless, opportunities remain. Current geopolitical tensions may spur increased focus on businesses oriented towards domestic and intra-Asia trade. We believe sectors likely to benefit from this shift include environmental and renewable energy assets, as well as digital infrastructure such as telecommunications towers and data centres. Despite softer equity activity, data from BNM underscores the resilience of Malaysias capital markets. In the first four months of 2025, net funds raised by the private sector reached RM46.2bn (excluding redemptions), up from RM34.2bn in the same period last year. This comprised RM1.4bn from new equity and warrant issuances (slightly below RM1.7bn in YTD 2024) and RM44.8bn from new debt securities (versus RM32.5bn previously). Non-NII remains a key lever for revenue diversification and ROE uplift We view non-NII as a critical driver for revenue diversification and ROE enhancement. Banks are increasingly turning to strategic partnerships and digital investments to strengthen this pillar. We believe that one promising development isHong Leong Banks new strategic alliance with Lombard Odier to scale its wealth management proposition. Through this collaboration, HLBs private banking clients will gain access to global CIO insights, advanced investment technology, and tailored product solutions  all geared toward building intergenerational wealth in a costefficient manner. Affin Bankis also making targeted investments, including new credit card and trade finance systems, to drive growth in higher-margin credit card business and fee-based income. Its recent partnership with MUFG is expected to open up opportunities in FX funding and create broader synergy benefits. Elsewhere,Alliance Bankis focusing on growing fee and treasury income through FX sales, trade fees, and wealth management offerings.Public Bankis intensifying cross-selling efforts with LPI Capital to boost bancassurance income, with a more meaningful uplift expected from FY26 onward.RHB, meanwhile, sees continued upside from its wealth and asset management businesses, bancassurance, and transactional banking segments. Operating Expenses Tightening Grip on Operating Expenses Banks are expected to maintain a tighter grip on overhead expenses, moving into CY25 and CY26, with operating expenses projected to rise by around 6.6% in both years. This comes after a 7.9% YoY increase recorded in CY24, largely driven by higher spending on personnel (9.3% YoY), marketing (15.3% YoY), and establishment costs (12.7% YoY). The increase was partially offset by a slight contraction in administrative and general expenses, which declined by 0.5% YoY. Operational efficiency remains a key focus, with banks continuing to balance cost control measures alongside long-term investments in technology and automation. While spending on IT infrastructure is expected to remain a structural cost driver, these investments are becoming increasingly essential as banks navigate a more complex digital landscape. Rising cyber threats, evolving data privacy regulations, and shifting customer expectations are pushing financial institutions to enhance their digital capabilities. The ongoing transition toward digital banking has made cybersecurity and system resilience critical components of operational stability. At the same time, demand for more sophisticated and convenient banking services is accelerating. According to BNM, e-payment adoption  including e-money and mobile banking  continues to grow, underpinned by increasing consumer awareness, education, and the availability of supporting infrastructure. In this environment, continued investment in digital channels is necessary not only to sustain service quality and operational efficiency but also to drive incremental revenue. We see opportunities for banks to deepen customer relationships through enhanced digital product offerings, potentially improving product holding ratios, fee-based income, and ROE. Recent bank briefings further highlight this strategic pivot.AMMBhas reaffirmed its focus on operational excellence through greater use of automation and digital tools.Affin Bankis channelling resources into new credit card and trade finance systems to improve margins and expand its fee income base. Meanwhile,Hong LeongBankspartnership with WeBank aims to streamline digital processes, particularly within auto financing and SME lending.Alliance Bank, on the other hand, is leveraging AI-driven solutions to support ESG reporting for newly listed companies. Technology-driven Investments to Keep Costs Elevated While these technology-driven investments are expected to keep operating costs elevated in the near termexemplified byMaybanksguidance under its M25 strategic roadmap they are crucial for building long-term operational resilience and efficiency.Alliance Bank, for instance, is guiding for a stable cost-to-income (CTI) ratio of 48% in FY26 despite a strong revenue outlook, reflecting its continued investments in both talent and technology. Notably,Hong Leong Bankhas demonstrated how technology-led efficiency gains can translate into superior cost management; the bank reported a CTI ratio of just 38.8%the lowest among peersattributed to disciplined cost controls and AI-driven process optimisation. Looking ahead, we expect banks to continue managing personnel costs by improving staff productivity rather than expanding headcount. Sector employment data points to a more subdued hiring environment, with job vacancies and total employment in the financial services sector declining by 0.4% and 1.2% YoY, respectively. Beyond human capital, we also see scope for banks to revisit the optimal size and distribution of their physical branch networks. With regional economic growth gaining momentum in markets such as Penang, Sabah, Sarawak, and Johor, several banks should reassess their physical presence to better align with growth opportunities. As a reference, there are currently 240 commercial bank branches in East Malaysia, compared to 144 each in Penang and Perak, 235 in Johor, 271 in Wilayah Persekutuan, and 402 in Selangor. Given the development potential in Sabah and Sarawak, resource reallocation toward these regions could enhance customer reach and support inclusive banking growth strategies. Despite the anticipated rise in operating expenses, we expect the banking sectors cost-toincome (CTI) ratio to remain broadly stable, averaging around 46.0% in CY25 and 46.3% in CY26  only marginally higher than the 45.8% recorded in CY24. This reflects our view that steady income growth will provide a sufficient buffer against elevated cost pressures, allowing banks to preserve overall efficiency. In our view, ongoing efforts to enhance productivity through digitalisation and cost optimisation initiatives will continue to play a critical role in containing structural cost escalation and supporting sustainable profitability across the sector. Asset Quality Asset Quality Trends Overall, asset quality across the banking sector remains resilient, supported by proactive risk management practices despite emerging sector-specific headwinds. Total impaired loans in the system continued to trend lower, underpinned by reductions in both consumer and business segments. However, certain banks did observe mild upticks in impairments within specific consumer portfolios  particularly inmortgages, hire purchases, and unsecured loans. That said, the increases were not material, and the system-wide gross impaired loans (GIL) ratio held steady at around 1.43%, supported by consumer and business GIL ratios of 1.09% and 4.33%, respectively. Among banks under our coverage, asset quality also showed continued YoY improvement. The average GIL ratio improved to 1.54% in 1QCY25, down from 1.67% in the prior year  closely tracking the broader industry trend. New impaired loan formations, however, registered a modest yearly and sequential uptick, as observed in 1Q 2025, attributed to pockets of stress in the consumer segment. Nevertheless, most banks reported stable and low GIL ratios in their latest results.Public BankandHong Leong Bank, in particular, continued to lead in asset quality, posting GIL ratios of 0.53% and 0.57%, respectively. Nonetheless, several banks flagged emerging areas of concern that are being closely monitored. For example,AffinBankhighlighted a rise inmortgageimpairments linked to cost-of-living pressures, while its Enterprise Banking Stage 2 loans remained elevated due to delayed payments from buyers, weaker export activity, and tighter liquidity conditions. Alliance Bank also pointed out some signs of stress in its unsecured lending portfolio. On the business side, exposure to external tariffs has been identified as a potential risk, though banks generally view their direct exposure to the US  estimated at 34% of total loans  as manageable. Still, there is growing attention on second-order effects, such as reduced investor flows or delays in corporate capital expenditure. In response, banks have been proactively engaging with clients to assess risks and implement early intervention strategies where needed. Improvements in Provisions and Credit Charges Provisioning trends across the banking sector continue to improve, supported by reductions in Stage 2 and Stage 3 Lifetime Expected Credit Losses (ECL), according to BNM. This trend is also evident among banks under our coverage, with 1Q provisions showing YoY improvement. On a quarterly basis, provisions also improved, driven notably byHong Leong Banks net allowance writeback of RM387mn following an MOA release of RM399mn. Looking ahead, we expect the improvement in provisions to continue, underpinned by more optimistic credit cost guidance from several banks. We project gross credit costs to ease further to 27.9 bps in CY2025 and 26.3 bps in CY2026.CIMB, for example, has revised its credit cost guidance lower from 3040 bps to 2535 bps, whileAMMBexpects net credit costs to remain below 30 bps in FY26. The only notable exception isRHB, which anticipates a slight uptick in credit costs following downward revisions to GDP forecasts. Nevertheless, RHB has intensified its domestic recovery efforts to mitigate the impact. That said, while the overall outlook on credit costs remains optimistic, there are still some downside risks, particularly from potential Macroeconomic Variables (MEV) adjustments if macro data turns out weaker-than-expected. Despite lower provisioning trends, banks also continue to maintain sizeable management overlays as precautionary buffers against macroeconomic uncertainties. As of the latest data,Maybankholds the largest overlay at RM1.8bn, followed byPublic Bank(RM1.2bn),AMMB(RM439mn), andRHB(RM317mn). While there have been some selective writebacks, we estimate that the total accumulated management overlays across banks under our coverage still stand at approximately RM6.0bn, down from RM7.8bn as of June 2024. Going forward, we anticipate banks will continue to reallocate or partially unwind these overlays, depending on how economic conditions evolve in 2025. That said, preserving a healthy loan loss coverage (LLC) ratio remains a key focus. We note that the average LLC for banks under coverage has declined to 102.5% as of the latest quarter from 113.3% in 1QCY24. Among those with LLC ratios below 100% areAMMB, RHB Bank, Hong Leong Bank,andAffin Bank, highlighting the need for continued prudence in managing provisioning buffers. Capital Adequate Buffers and Strong Capital Ratios The Malaysian banking sector continues demonstrating healthy capital positions, with buffers well above regulatory thresholds, underscoring the systems resilience and capacity to support future growth. As of April 2025, the industrys Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio stood at 14.3%, while the Total Capital Ratio was 17.7%. Liquidity also remains ample, with the Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) at a robust 156%, providing further assurance amid a backdrop of external uncertainties. All banks under our coverage are expected to maintain sound capital buffers, supported by prudent balance sheet management and conservative provisioning strategies. Among recent developments,Alliance Bankannounced a proposed RM600mn renounceable rights issue in March 2025 to strengthen its capital position. The issue, priced at RM3.33 per rights share on the basis of two rights shares for every 17 existing shares, received overwhelming shareholder approval with 99.9% votes in favour at its EGM. The move is expected to raise the groups CET1 ratio by 1.1 percentage points to 13.5%, narrowing the gap with the industry average. Similarly,Affin Bankopted to issue bonus shares in lieu of cash dividends, signalling its focus on retaining capital to support growth initiatives. Dividends and Payout Policies Despite macroeconomic headwinds, most banks appear well-positioned to sustain their dividend policies, supported by healthy earnings generation and capital strength.AMMB, for instance, has raised its dividend payout commitment to 50% under its new strategic planup from the previous 40%and targets to sustain this into FY26, given its improved capital trajectory. Despite its ongoing rights issue,Alliance Bankhas maintained its dividend payout guidance of 4050%, reflecting confidence in its capital position post-issuance.CIMBcontinues to adhere to its 4060% dividend policy and has consistently delivered a 55% payout in recent years. The group has also expressed openness to returning additional capital to shareholders through special dividends, depending on its capital position and growth needs. Valuation and Recommendation Attractive Valuations Persist Amid Outperformance The KLFIN Index has continued to outperform the broader FBM KLCI, in line with our overweight stance on the banking sector. Despite this outperformance, we believe the sector remains fundamentally attractive from a valuation perspective. The sector currently trades at a forward FY25 P/B ratio of 0.97x, supported by an industry ROE of approximately 10.1% and an average dividend yield of 5.3%. Most banksexcluding Affin Bankare trading at or below one standard deviation below their 10-year P/BV mean, reinforcing the view that valuations remain undemanding and offer upside potential. Stabilising Foreign Shareholding Trends Following a marginal dip in foreign shareholding during 1Q 2025, most banks have reported a recovery in foreign investor interest through April and May. That said, foreign shareholding levels remain below historical peaks, indicating room for further inflows. Maybank and CIMB remain the key proxies for global investors seeking exposure to Malaysia's financial sector and broader macroeconomic fundamentals.Maybank'sforeign shareholding rose to 19.5% in May 2025 from 19.2% in April, whileCIMBsaw its foreign ownership peak at 36.1% in late 2024 before easing slightly to 33.9% as of May 2025.Public Bankalso experienced a meaningful rise in foreign shareholding from 19.0% to 26.9% by the end of 2024 before dipping to 25.6% in May 2025. Foreign Rotation Into Mid-cap Banks We also observe a slight shift in foreign investor positioning, with increasing interest in midsized banks.RHB Bank, in particular, has seen a steady rise in foreign shareholding  from 13.97% in May 2024 to 20.45%  reflecting renewed investor confidence.AMMBhas also attracted consistent foreign inflows, with shareholding levels increasing from 20% in December 2023 to 33.5% in March 2025. This momentum is supported by its earnings recovery, strengthened capital buffers post-1MDB resolution, and improving dividend track record However, foreign shareholding inHong Leong Bankhas tapered recently, potentially due to concerns around softer contributions from its associate, Bank of Chengdu. Smaller banks likeAlliance BankandAffin Bankhave also reported a slight decline in foreign interest. For Alliance Bank, earlier foreign interest may have been driven by market speculation around a potential M&A with DBS Bank, while Affin Bank's earlier momentum may have been linked to optimism surrounding its new strategic shareholder  the Sarawak state government  and the anticipated uplift in earnings. Earnings Growth Outlook Remains Resilient for 2025 Despite lingering macroeconomic uncertainties, the earnings outlook for the Malaysian banking sector in 2025 remains constructive. Sector earnings are projected to expand by 4.6% to RM37.3bn, underpinned by sound fundamentals and continued operational resilience. Loan growth is expected to accelerate by 5.7%, supported by healthy demand across both retail and business segments. With margins likely to stay relatively stable, NII is forecasted to rise by 4.3%. Islamic banking contributions are also set to see healthy momentum, with growth anticipated at 7.0%driven by deeper market penetration and rising customer adoption of Shariahcompliant offerings. Non-NII is poised for an 8.7% uplift, reflecting stronger fee income traction and broader diversification across bancassurance, wealth, and digital services. Asset quality is projected to remain broadly stable, with the sector's credit cost expected to ease slightly to 27.9 bps, further cushioning profitability. Meanwhile, robust capital and liquidity buffers position banks to pursue growth opportunities and withstand potential external shocks. Overall, we maintain a positive stance on the sector's earnings trajectory heading into 2025. With these positive indicators, we maintain anOVERWEIGHTstance on the sector. Our top recommendations for the sector are: 1) CIMB, in our view, remains a compelling stock pick, supported by its new Forward30 Strategic Plan, which emphasises capital reallocation and optimisation to strengthen its ASEAN footprint amid a volatile macro landscape. Asset quality continues to exceed expectations, with loan loss charges improving to 26 bps in 1QFY25 and management lowering credit cost guidance to 2535 bps while maintaining prudent provisioning through additional overlays. Foreign investor interest remains stable, with shareholding rising to 33.9%, aided by CIMBs strong ESG credentials and improving financial performance. Backed by a solid CET1 ratio of 14.6%, CIMB has delivered special dividends for two consecutive years and maintains a sustainable 4050% dividend payout policy. Trading at 1.0x FY25 P/B around its 10-year average and below peersCIMB offers an attractive valuation upside. 2) Public Bank, because earnings remain heavily anchored in its domestic, consumercentric operations, retail and hire purchase segments contribute 55% to group PBT and 94% of overall profits derived locally. The banks strategic focus on domestic retail and SME lending aligns well with expectations of Malaysia-led macro growth in 2025. PBB also benefits from sustained unit trust income amid global market volatility. Asset quality remains a standout, with a low impaired loan ratio of 0.5%, high loan loss coverage at 159.9%, and a strong RM1.2bn management overlay. Operational efficiency is a key strength, reflected in a lean 35% CTI ratio vs peers 43.6%. Currently trading near -1 SD of its 10-year P/B mean, PBB offers a compelling valuation upside, especially with improving foreign investor interest foreign shareholding rose significantly in 2024 before a slight pullback in 2025, leaving room for re-entry supported by its robust fundamentals and high ROE. 3) Hong Leong Bankas it continues to outperform peers, particularly in consumer and SME loans, with 3Q FY25 loan growth reaching 7.2%, surpassing industry averages and management's guidance. This fuelled solid topline growth, including a 34.1% YoY rise in non-NII. Core pre-BOCD profit rose 24.2% YoY in 9MFY25, supported by healthy contributions from wealth management, global markets, and SME banking. Overseas, HLBB posted robust loan growth in Singapore and Vietnam. Asset quality remains exceptional, with a low GIL ratio of 0.57%, though LLC declined due to a RM399mn MOA release. Strategic partnerships with Lombard Odier and WeBank are expected to further elevate HLBB's wealth and digital banking propositions. Potential Risks and Challenges While the outlook for the banking sector remains broadly positive, there are several risks worth monitoring. A key concern is the potential deterioration in asset quality should external shocks emerge, especially in a still-fragile global economic environment. Softer contributions from regional operations may also materialise if the ringgit strengthens further, eroding foreign-sourced earnings. On the domestic front, rising competition in loan pricing could lead to some margin compression, while sustained investments in technology and talent may keep overhead costs elevated in the near term. Additionally, while banks remain committed to dividend payouts, these could come under pressure as the sector prepares for the potential capital impact from the implementation of Basel 3.5. That said, we believe the sector is still well-supported by solid fundamentals, with stable asset quality, healthy capital buffers, and decent income growth cushioning against these risks. Source: TA Research - 26 Jun 2025

The food inflation rate was 1.1% in May, while healthcare costs climbed 2.7%. Earlier this month, economists from a separate Bloomberg survey cut their outlook for both headline and core inflation for this year by at least 0.4 percentage points due to slower global demand and declining global oil prices. Official estimates project the countrys core inflation to average between 0.5% and 1.5% this year. The next central bank meeting is scheduled to be held next month. (Bloomberg) Powell Headed for Congressional Grilling Over Feds Rate Hold Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell will have two chances this week to explain to lawmakers why he and most of his fellow policymakers seem resolved to continue holding interest rates steady at least until September, ignoring President Donald Trumps persistent calls to lower borrowing costs. The Fed chair will testify before the House Financial Services Committee at 10 a.m. Tuesday, and at the same time on Wednesday before the Senate Banking Committee. The appearances come less than a week after officials agreed to keep rates unchanged for a fourth consecutive meeting. They also follow the recent US attack on Iran, which escalated fears of surging oil prices and risks to the global economy. (Bloomberg) US Manufacturing Expands While Prices Paid, Received Accelerate US manufacturing grew at a steady pace so far in June, partly reflecting stronger employment growth, while two measures of inflation accelerated to the highest levels since July 2022. The S&P Global flash June factory purchasing managers index held at 52, the highest since February, according to data released Monday. Figures above 50 indicate growth. The group's index of prices paid for materials climbed 5.4 points, the most in four years, to 70. A nearly similar increase in prices received indicates producers are passing along higher costs including import duties. While domestic demand has strengthened, notably in manufacturing, to encourage higher employment, this in part reflects a boost from stock building, in turn often linked to concerns over higher prices and supply issues resulting from tariffs, Chris Williamson, chief business economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence, said in a statement. (Bloomberg) US Home Resales Stay Sluggish on Affordability Constraints US previously owned home sales rose slightly in May to a still-sluggish pace that continues to show a housing market constrained by poor affordability. Contract closings increased 0.8% to an annualized rate of 4.03mn last month, just the second advance this year, according to data released Monday by the National Association of Realtors. That compared with the 3.95mn median estimate in a Bloomberg survey of economists. It was the weakest May sales pace since 2009. The resale market, which historically makes up about 90% of total home sales, looks set to languish for the foreseeable future without some letup in financing costs or downturn in prices. Compared with a year ago, existing-home sales were down 4% on an unadjusted basis. (Bloomberg) Eurozone Private Sector Near Stagnation on Global Uncertainty The euro areas private sector barely grew in June, remaining in limbo as erratic US trade policy and geopolitical conflicts leave companies in the dark on whats next. The Composite Purchasing Managers Index by S&P Global held at 50.2, remaining just above the 50-threshold separating growth from contraction, data Monday showed. Economists had anticipated an acceleration to 50.5. Services made it back to the vital 50 level as well, while manufacturing stayed at 49.4  failing to grow for a 36th month. The eurozone economy is struggling to gain momentum, said Cyrus de la Rubia, an economist at Hamburg Commercial Bank. For six months now, growth has been minimal. The data suggest economic output in the second quarter will be constrained. That would match the view of economists, who predicted in the latest Bloomberg survey that gross domestic product will stagnate in the three months through June  held back by massive uncertainty over US President Donald Trumps tariff push and wars in Ukraine and the Middle East. (Bloomberg) UK Private Sector Grows at Fastest Pace Since Trump Tariffs Hit The UK private sector expanded at a marginally faster pace in June, helping ease worries of an economic contraction in the second quarter, according to a closely watched survey. S&P Global said its purchasing managers index rose to 50.7 in June, up from 50.3 in May and above analyst expectations, according to provisional estimates published Monday. The reading is slightly above the 50-threshold indicating expansion and marks a recovery to levels last seen in March, just before Donald Trumps tariff announcements hit confidence and slashed exports to the US. The report will provide some relief for the Labour government, which is struggling to deliver sustained economic growth against a backdrop of higher taxes, trade wars and now an escalating conflict in the Middle East. (Bloomberg) Source:TA Research- 24 Jun 2025

KUALA LUMPUR (June 11): A developer of data centres planned in the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ) has secured RM15 billion in financing from a group of lenders. Singapore-based DayOne secured the multi-currency financing, comprising RM7.5 billion onshore Islamic facility and a US$1.7 billion offshore term loan, from a consortium of local and international banks. Its anchor data centres will be in Nusajaya and Kempas Tech Parks, both within the JS-SEZ. The financing involves CIMB Group Holdings Bhd (KL:CIMB), Crédit Agricole, DBS Bank Ltd, Malayan Banking Bhd (KL:MAYBANK), Standard Chartered, United Overseas Bank (UOB), and Oversea-Chinese Banking Corporation Ltd (OCBC). Minister of Investment, Trade and Industry Minister Tengku Datuk Seri Zafrul Abdul Aziz witnessed a signing ceremony for the loan syndication on Wednesday. The transaction is also the largest syndicated green Islamic facility in Malaysia. This landmark transaction represents Maybanks largest Islamic financing commitment to the data-centre sector, said its chief executive officer Khairussaleh Ramli. Maybank is the joint shariah adviser, joint coordinator, mandated lead arranger and bookrunner, joint hedge coordinator, and joint green financing and loan coordinator for the financing facility. Structured under the Green Loan Principles, part of the proceeds from the dual-tranche financing is for refinancing and to support capital expenditure for DayOnes data centres in Johor, that meet or are expected to meet LEED Gold or higher certification. The development in the JS-SEZ represents a critical bridge between Malaysia and Singapores digital ecosystems, positioning Asean as a premier destination for cloud computing and digital services, UOB, a joint coordinator, mandated lead arranger, underwriter and bookrunner, said in a separate statement. DayOne has data centres across Asia, including in Singapore, Indonesia, Bangkok, Hong Kong, and Tokyo. By jointly financing DayOnes data centre in the JS-SEZ, we are not only supporting Malaysias digital economy but also strengthening the connectivity backbone that enables businesses from both sides of the Causeway to thrive in the digital age, said UOB Malaysia CEO Ng Wei Wei. We supported DayOne with one of the highest underwriting commitments, reflecting our commitment to fostering a sustainable future that aligns with our leadership in responsible finance, said Tan Chor Sen, chief executive officer of OCBC Malaysia. OCBC is also one of the joint coordinators, mandated lead arrangers and bookrunners, and green financing coordinators and joint shariah advisers for the facility. Source: TheEdge - 12 Jun 2025

Immediate index support stays at 1,490, while stronger supports can be found at 1,465 and 1,444. Immediate resistance remains at 1,564 with next upside hurdles seen at the recent high of 1,586, followed by 1,610 ahead. Asian Markets Climb Following US-China Trade Agreement Stocks in Asia climbed on Wednesday following news that the US and China had reached an agreement to move forward on trade after two days of high-level talks in London. Both parties agreed to a framework on implementing the consensus reached in the prior round of talks in Geneva, and it will now need to be approved by respective leaders in Washington and Beijing. According to US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, issues regarding Chinese restrictions on rare-earth exports will be resolved as the framework is implemented upon which US restrictions on the selling of advanced tech to China would also be rolled back. In economic news, Japan's wholesale inflation rose 3.2% year-on-year in May due to falling import costs for raw materials. Japans Nikkei 225 gained 0.55% to 38,421.19 and the Topix inched higher by 0.09% to 2,788.72. In mainland, the Shanghai Composite Index added 0.52% to 3,402.32, while Hong Kongs Hang Seng Index advanced 0.84% to finish at 24,366.94. Meanwhile in Australia, the S&P/ASX 200 rose 0.06% to end at 8,592.10, while South Koreas jumped 1.23% to 2,907.04. Wall Street Drops as Traders Assess Inflation Data and Trade Talks Wall Street's major indexes declined overnight as traders reacted to a weaker-than-expected inflation report and evaluated a US-China framework aimed at reviving their trade truce. The Dow Jones Industrial Average closed virtually unchanged at 42,865.77. The S&P 500 lost 0.27% to end the day at 6,022.24, while the Nasdaq Composite fell 0.5% to 19,615.88. Fresh economic data showed the annual rate of inflation edged up to 2.4% in May, from 2.3% in April, matching the expectations of economists polled by The Wall Street Journal. President Trump responded to the data by repeating calls for the Federal Reserve to slash interest rates by a full percentage point. Meanwhile, discussions between U.S. and Chinese officials have been a key focus this week for traders who remain on edge regarding trade policy. Officials reached a consensus after two days of talks in London but said that they will seek approval on the framework from the U.S. and Chinese presidents before implementing it. As part of the framework, China would approve the exports of rare earth minerals while the U.S. would roll back restrictions on the sale of advanced technology to China. Even as the framework is being finalized, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said that U.S. tariffs on Chinese imports will not change from their current levels. (The Edge, Bernama) SST Revision, Expansion for Equitable Taxation, Protection of Vulnerable Groups The sales and service tax (SST) revision and expansion exemplify the governments commitment to progressive and equitable taxation that strengthens fiscal position while protecting those who need it most, said Finance Minister II Datuk Seri Amir Hamzah Azizan. The minister said that as the government works towards the country escaping the middleincome trap, progressive taxation becomes an important tool for sustainable and inclusive growth. The approach is deliberately targeted where every day Malaysians continue to enjoy zero percent sales tax on essential and everyday goods: chicken, rice, vegetables, cooking oil, and medicine, for instance. In addition, basic construction materials remain untaxed, and small businesses with revenue below key thresholds are protected through carefully calibrated exemptions, he said. On Monday, the government announced that it would implement a targeted revision of the sales tax rate and expansion of the service taxs scope, effective from July 1. The sales tax rate will remain unchanged for essential goods consumed by the people, while a rate of either 5% or 10% will be imposed on non-essential or discretionary goods. The scope of the service tax will be extended to include new services such as rental or leasing, construction, finance, private healthcare, education, and beauty services. KUALA LUMPUR (May 28): Malaysias central bank said on Wednesday it has imposed fines totalling nearly RM5 million on four lenders for failing to comply with financial services laws. HSBC and its Islamic arm were together fined RM3.26 million, while Malayan Banking Bhds (KL:MAYBANK) unit Maybank Islamic Bhd was fined RM1.2 million, Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM) said in a statement. A penalty of RM493,500 was also levied on Bank Pembangunan Malaysia Bhd. All four banks paid their fines between March and April this year. HSBC did not comply with customer due diligence requirements, which BNM said was found during an on-site examination. The examination revealed a lack of understanding of the requirements for identifying beneficial ownership, the central bank noted. Banks are required to identify and take reasonable measures to verify beneficial owners, which would allow an assessment of their exposures to money laundering or terrorism financing risks and take appropriate mitigation measures. In addition, the measures aim to prevent individuals from misusing corporate vehicles to conceal illegal assets by maintaining a legitimate front in the financial system. HSBC also found lapses in sanctions screening requirements during its own review in 2023. It was discovered that customers were onboarded without proper sanctions screening due to staff oversight, ineffective maker-checker functions, and inadequate system capabilities, BNM said. Bank Pembangunan was also found to be in non-compliance with customer due diligence and sanctions screening requirements. It was caused by the staffs inadequate understanding of the specific requirements related to beneficial ownerships and gaps in its sanctions screening process, which led to the failure to conduct sanctions screening on its existing customers without delay, BNM said. Maybank Islamic, meanwhile, breached a law on submission of information to the Central Credit Reference Information System more commonly known as CCRIS. The lender did not properly submit information on three customers, which later affected the borrowers credit records, BNM said. Banks are required to submit timely, accurate and complete information in the CCRIS. Accurate reporting in the CCRIS by financial institutions is critical to enable fair credit assessment by lenders on prospective borrowers, BNM stressed. Source: TheEdge - 29 May 2025

Excluding biomedical manufacturing, industrial output registered an annual growth of 8.1% compared to 6.0% increase in the prior month. Bolstered by more maintenance, repair and overhaul jobs from commercial airlines, transport engineering output surged 22.9%. Electronic output advanced 15.2% with strong growth in consumer electronics. Precision engineering output climbed only 1.6% due to a fall in the machinery and systems segment. (The Edge, Bernama) Trump delays EU tariffs until July 9, European markets rally US President Donald Trump backed away from his threat to impose 50% tariffs on imports from the European Union (EU) next month, restoring a July 9 deadline to allow for talks between Washington and the 27-nation bloc to produce a deal. European assets rallied on Monday. Trump had said on Friday he was recommending a 50% tariff effective from June 1, expressing frustration that trade negotiations with the EU were not moving quickly enough. The threat roiled global financial markets and intensified a trade war that has been punctuated by frequent changes in tariff policies towards US trading partners and allies. The US president's softened stance two days later marked another temporary reprieve in his erratic trade policy, even if the latest whipsawing in decision making reminded policymakers and investors how quickly circumstances could change. Trump, who has repeatedly expressed disdain for the EU and its treatment of the US on trade, relented after European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen told him on Sunday that the EU needed more time to come to an agreement. She asked him during a call to delay the tariffs until July, the deadline he had originally set when he announced new tariffs in April. Trump told reporters he had granted the request. Anything is possible, Kashkari said on Monday in an interview on Bloomberg Television in Tokyo. If trade deals are struck between the US and other nations over the next few months, that should provide a lot of the clarity we are looking for, he added. While the US economy entered 2025 on solid footing, US President Donald Trumps tariffs and substantial changes to the countrys immigration policy have prompted businesses to rethink investment plans. Kashkari and other central bank officials, including Chicago Fed chief Austan Goolsbee, have said the bar remains high for cutting interest rates in the near term. (Bloomberg) Trump says hes eyeing Russia sanctions, calls Putin crazy President Donald Trump said he was absolutely considering new sanctions against Russia as Ukraine said Moscow launched its largest drone barrage of the war during a third night of strikes. Trumps comments, made to reporters in New Jersey on Sunday came as he grows visibly frustrated with Russian President Vladimir Putin and the state of talks meant to deliver a ceasefire in Ukraine. The US president has repeatedly threatened new sanctions in the past without following through. The Kremlin on Monday said that Putin is acting to protect national security. Responding to Trumps comments, spokesman Dmitry Peskov said the start of talks between Ukraine and Russia means theres emotional overload of absolutely everyone and emotional reactions, according to the Interfax news service. (Bloomberg) EU plans to fast track trade talks with US amid tariff fight The European Union said it agreed to accelerate negotiations with the US to avoid a transatlantic trade war, signaling a more amicable approach just days after President Donald Trump criticized the bloc for taking advantage of the US and slow-walking talks. Theres now a new impetus for the negotiations, Paula Pinho, a spokeswoman for the European Commission, told reporters on Monday, a day after Commission President Ursula von der Leyen spoke with Trump by phone. They agreed both to fast track the trade negotiations and to stay in close contact. Following the call, Trump extended the deadline to hit the EU with 50% tariffs by more than a month to July 9 to allow for more negotiations. Talks so far have been beset with a multitude of problems, with no clear path to finding a middle ground that will appease them both. The Europeans have complained that its not clear what the US wants or even who speaks for the American president, and the US has said the EU unfairly targets US companies with lawsuits and regulations. Have a look at: Expect NIM compression in subsequent quarters.Management believes that rate cuts will compress future NIMs. The main means to offset will be replacing pricier money market deposits (which MAY has been hoarding for several quarters) with cheaper local retail FDs. MAY will also be looking at drawing down on borrowings, and for further portfolio rebalancing/funding opportunities in SG. A - 25bps OPR cut will impact NIMs by 3bps. Etiqa faces some drag.Etiqa's 1QFY25 contribution was dragged by weaker investment income from SG. Underwriting income remains solid. Regardless, if we observe NOII as a whole, MAY's large dependence on forex gains implies that further forex volatility is still an overall positive for the Group (at least, from a pure NOII standpoint) - even though Etiqa's SG investment income will take a hit. Elevated CIR may persist.Management has stated tech investments will continue post-M25, which implies that CIR may not normalise to its previous 45%level range just yet. Asset quality situation is fairly stable.The Group is not expecting too many negative surprises. Only RM7.3b (1.1% of total loans) have exposure to US trade, with only RM1b of exposure related to borrowers that have 20% revenue from US. Local residential mortgage GILs seem stable, though hire purchase loans are seeing some uptick. Loan growth may come in at the lower end of guidance.Management does not sound overly optimistic on loan growth prospects, stating that the corporate segment is seeing a lot more competition from development financing. KUALA LUMPUR (May 26): Malayan Banking Bhd (KL:MAYBANK), the countrys biggest by bank by assets, said its net profit edged up 4% in the first quarter from a year earlier, as income from interests andinsuranceoffset a decline in non-interest income. Net profit for the three months ended March 31, 2025 (1QFY2025) was RM2.59 billion, or 21.45 sen per share, Maybank said in an exchange filing. Year-on-year (y-o-y), net interest income was up 0.8% to RM5.29 billion, while non-interest income was down 27% to RM2.87 billion. Provisions for potential bad loans and investment losses also fell. However, the bank insurance/takaful service result for the quarter more than doubled to RM471.4 million. Maybank also reiterated its target for a return on equity of at least 11.3% for FY2025. The group will continue to double down on penetration of its extensive customer base through focus on segments, cross selling endeavours, and leveraging ecosystem partnerships regionally, Maybank said in a filing on Monday. It added that the focus will also be on its super growth areas of wealth management, mid-market cap, non-retail, and bancassurance segments, while tapping on global market flows. Further, the bank said that it will seek to maintain sound liquidity, robust asset quality, and strong capital levels to support disciplined asset growth. The volatility and uncertainty surrounding potential trade disruptions, however, may have an impact on the growth and performance of the group, as a result of slower economic growth, a wait-and-see stance in investments and capital raising activities, and from financial markets volatility, it added. In 1QFY2024, Maybanks gross loans were lifted by an 8% increase in Malaysia, 5.9% increase in Singapore, and a 0.8% increase y-o-y in Indonesia. Meanwhile, deposits expanded y-o-y by 5.1%, led by growth across its Singapore (17.9%) and Malaysian (4.4%) markets. The banks net interest margin  a measure of profitability from interests charged on loans after deducting returns paid to depositors  stabilised at 2.04%, supported by improved net fund-based income, which increased 2.3% to RM4.95 billion. In terms of asset quality, gross impaired loans  debts deemed unrecoverable as a percentage of total loans  improved by five basis points to 1.27%, while loan loss coverage slipped to 122.9%, from 127.3% in 1QFY2024. Maybanks common equity tier 1 capital  a measure of a banks capital strength based on the highest quality of regulatory capital  was 14.88%, and its total capital ratio stood at 17.96%. Meanwhile, its liquidity coverage ratio remained stable at 135.7%, above the regulatory requirement of 100%. No dividend was announced. The lender usually announces dividends with its second- and fourth-quarter results. Maybank chief executive officer Datuk Khairussaleh Ramli said the bank expects continued growth in its markets. He added that they remain focused on completing M25 by strengthening the core, speeding up digital transformation, and embedding sustainability. Shares in Maybank rose four sen or 0.4% to close at RM9.92 on Monday, valuing the bank at RM119.84 billion. Year-to-date, shares in the bank have slipped 3%. Source: TheEdge - 27 May 2025

Market Review The FBMKLCI ended lower (-0.1%) to 1,534.3, mirroring the cautious sentiment seen across Wall Street and European markets last Friday, as resurfacing trade and tariff risks weighed on global investor confidence. Market jitters were reignited following US President Donald Trump's threat to impose a 50 percent tariff on European Union imports, raising concerns over a potential reescalation of global trade tensions. Although the implementation has since been deferred to July 9 from the initial June 1 timeline, investor sentiment remains fragile amid uncertainty surrounding the broader trajectory of international trade relations. Among the key index constituents, significant selling pressure were observed in YTLP (-4.4%), YTL (-2.7%) and MISC (-2.4%). Sector wise, Technology (-1.3%), Transportation & Logistics (-0.8%) and Energy (-0.7%) are the top 3 laggards. Overall market breadth turned bearish, with 595 decliners outnumbered 338 gainers. Asian equity markets closed mixed on Monday amid renewed trade tensions, following remarks from U.S. President Donald Trump suggesting the potential imposition of a 25% tariff on smartphones manufactured outside the United States. The proposed tariffs, which could be implemented by the end of next month, would target major global producers including Apple and Samsung Electronics Co., adding a layer of uncertainty for the broader technology supply chain and investor sentiment. SHANGHAI CI closed flat while the HSI tumbled 1.4%. European equities opened the week on a positive footing, buoyed by renewed risk appetite as investors responded to the deferment of tariff escalation by U.S. President Donald Trump. The proposed 50% tariffs on European Union goods, originally slated for implementation on June 1, have now been postponed to July 9 following a conciliatory call with EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen. This development appears to have triggered selective repositioning across key sectors, with market participants viewing the delay as a tactical reprieve that temporarily reduces geopolitical trade headwinds. The European STOXX 50 turned positive and closed 1.3% higher. US market was closed in conjunction with the celebration of Memorial Day. Asian Markets Rebound on Trade Hopes Asian markets rebounded on Tuesday, driven by optimism surrounding trade negotiations and economic shifts in the region. Traders in Asia are staying focused on the outcome of US trade negotiations with India and Japan after talks with China on lowering tariffs boosted optimism. India is discussing a US trade deal structured in three tranches and expects to reach an interim agreement before July, while Japans finance minister said hes arranging a bilateral meeting with US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent this week to discuss topics including currency matters. On economic front, China cut benchmark lending rates for the first time since October on Tuesday, after Beijing announced sweeping monetary easing measures earlier this month to support the broad economy. Separately, Australias central bank also cut its policy rate to 3.85%, its lowest level since May 2023, as inflation concerns in the country continue to recede, giving room for the bank to ease monetary policy. In mainland, the Shanghai Composite Index gained 0.38% to 3,380.48, while Hong Kongs Hang Seng Index jumped 1.49% to 23,681.48. Japans Nikkei 225 inched higher by 0.08% to 37,529.49 and the Topix ended flat at 2,738.83. In Australia, the S&P/ASX rose 0.58% to 8,343.30, while South Koreas Kospi inched lower by 0.06% to 2,601.80. Wall Street Falls Amid Tax Reform Vote Uncertainty Wall Street's major indexes declined overnight as traders closely monitored a pivotal vote in Washington regarding U.S. President Donald Trump's extensive tax reform plan. The Dow Jones Industrial Average lost 0.27% to close at 42,677.24. The S&P 500 shed 0.39% to 5,940.46, while the Nasdaq Composite fell 0.38% to 19,142.71. President Donald Trump traveled to Capitol Hill, seeking to persuade Republican lawmakers to pass a sweeping taxcut bill, which analysts estimate will possibly add USD3 trillion to USD5 trillion to the federal government's USD36.2 trillion in debt. Traders are worried the bill will lead to the U.S. budget deficit growing at a faster pace than previously expected. Several Fed policymakers spoke on the state of the US economy throughout the day, including St. Louis Fed president Alberto Musalem. He said tariffs could still cause a "significant" impact despite the latest US-China trade reprieve. Traders dumped tech stocks, which had led the run over the past six days. Nvidia lost 0.9%. Advanced Micro Devices, Meta Platforms, Apple and Microsoft were also lower.
