Downside Pressure Rises Amid External Uncertainty The FTSE Bursa Malaysia Kuala Lumpur Composite Index (FBM KLCI) endured a volatile trading week, mirroring fragile sentiment across global markets as escalating Middle East tensions weighed heavily on risk appetite. Concerns over potential oil supply disruptions and broader economic instability stemming from intensifying military conflict involving the US, Israel, and Iran triggered sharp selloffs in the local benchmark. Losses, however, were partly cushioned by bargain-hunting as selective buying emerged after steep pullbacks. In the first week of March, the FBM KLCI edged up 1.45 points, or 0.08%, to close at 1,718.06. Gains in Petronas Chemicals (RM1.32), Press Metal Aluminium Holdings (68 sen), MISC (63 sen), and PPB Group (26 sen) offset declines in YTL Corp (-11 sen) and MRDIY (-9 sen). Average daily traded volume rose to 3.46 billion shares versus 2.75 billion the prior week, while average daily traded value increased to RM3.74 billion from RM3.6 billion. Geopolitical Shockwaves The FBM KLCI was the only index among our tracked benchmarks to hold steady despite coordinated US-Israel strikes on Iran, targeting nuclear and missile facilities. The attack sidelined ongoing nuclear containment talks and escalated simmering tensions into open conflict. While regional instability had been anticipated since last Junes 12-day clash and the US naval buildup in the Middle East, the timingduring the fasting monthwas unexpected and controversial. The strikes killed Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and several senior officials, creating a leadership vacuum that could either open space for de-escalation or fuel aggressive power plays by clerics, the Revolutionary Guard, and political elites. Proxy groups such as Hezbollah and the Houthis may also act out to assert loyalty and influence succession dynamics. Iran is currently governed by a three-person leadership council comprising President Masoud Pezeshkian, judiciary chief Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei, and Ayatollah Alireza Arafi. The Assembly of Experts, an 88-member panel, will soon decide on the next Supreme Leader. The eventual appointmentamid external pressure, including demands from President Trump to influence the processcould shape the trajectory of war and diplomacy. War Duration and Global Risks The length of the conflict remains uncertain but we dont expect it to end anytime soon. President Trump is keen to end hostilities quickly, as prolonged US casualties ahead of midterm elections would be politically damaging. Financing an extended war would also strain the US economy, especially amid rising momentum for dedollarisation. The outcome hinges on whether Iran secures direct support from Russia and China. While both powers face their own challenges, sustained backing could prolong the war and risk escalation into a wider global conflict. Energy Market Fallout The closure of the Strait of Hormuza critical global shipping chokepointhas already disrupted supply chains. Prolonged closure could push crude oil prices towards USD150 per barrel, triggering global energy shortages and risk-off financial conditions. For Malaysia, higher crude export prices provide some cushion, but rising subsidy burdens, weaker consumption, and external volatility can tilt the outlook toward stagflation. Inflation risks may outpace growth, limiting policy flexibility despite Malaysias net energy exporter status and fiscal buffers. Main Sectoral Impact Consumer sector will face rising fuel and logistics costs compressing margins. While consumer staplesNESTLE (Buy, TP:RM128.00), FFB (Buy, TP:RM3.20)andF&N (Buy, TP:RM40.50)remain resilient, export volumes and shipment costs pose challenges. Non-staple playersAMWAY (Sell, TP:RM5.33), BESHOM (Sell, TP:RM0.51)andPADINI (Buy, TP:RM2.30)face demand risks from weaker discretionary spending. Transportation sector, especially aviation and logistics are most exposed. For AAX (Not Rated), we estimate every USD1 increase in jet fuel could cut earnings by RM81mn, with limited hedging protection. Fuel surcharges may mitigate losses, but demand destruction from higher ticket prices could drive EBITDA losses into billions. Logistics firms, includingWPRTS (Buy, TP:RM7.40),face cost pressures and rerouting delays. We expect an 8% decline in transshipment and gateway volumes could reduce FY26 earnings by 13%, or 6% YoY. On the flip side, energy and commodities may benefit. Upstream oil producers (Hibiscus, Not Rated) and rig operatorsVELESTO (Sell, TP:RM0.26)stand to benefit if high oil prices sustain. Plantation players gain from biodiesel substitution, with every RM100/tonne increase in CPO prices lifting sector earnings by 3%. Power and Utilities will benefit as coal-fired IPPs enjoy positive fuel margins from rising coal prices. Sustained high gas/coal costs could accelerate demand for renewable and nuclear alternatives that will benefitSAMAIDEN (Buy, TP:RM1.96)andTENAGA (Buy, TP:RM18.00), respectively. FBMKLCI Target and Stock Picks Our 2026 Market Outlook maintains a base-case FBM KLCI target of 1,760, with best- and worst-case scenarios at 1,850 and 1,600, respectively. A successful neutralization of Iran could revive investment appetite, especially given Malaysias low foreign equity ownership (19.2% as of end-February). This limited foreign exposure also cushions downside risks, with domestic buying likely to support key levels atNovember 2025 low of 1,597 and April 2025 low of 1,386 before bouncing back. Historical precedent suggests war-related market shocks are short-lived. Note that during the US invasion of Iraq in 2003 that ended in 2011, the FBM KLCI began recovering within two months, while crude oil pressures eased after five months. In navigating the current geopolitical uncertainty, we believe investors should lean towards a defensive and domestically anchored strategy. The themes we highlighted earlier in the yearEnergy Transition, Digital Economy and Infrastructure, and Fundamentally Solid Blue Chipsremain highly relevant and provide resilience against external shocks. Within Energy Transition,TENAGA (Buy, TP:RM18.00) and MALAKOF (Buy, TP:RM1.29)stand out as defensive plays backed by long-term concessions. Tenagas revenues are supported by regulated tariffs and cost pass-through mechanisms, while government oversight and longterm contracts with independent power producers reinforce its stability. Malakoffs cash flows are underpinned by long-term Power Purchase Agreements with Tenaga, and the company is well positioned to participate in the Energy Commissions upcoming gas capacity tender by leveraging sites from expiring plants. The ongoing turmoil is unlikely to derail Malaysias investment momentum in the digital economy and infrastructure, which continue to underpin productivity gains and strengthen the countrys competitiveness in the region. We maintainCDB (Buy, TP:RM4.35), TM (Buy, TP:RM8.80), GAMUDA (Buy, TP:RM5.98),andSIMEPROP (Buy, TP:RM2.10)as key buys within this theme. Both CDB and TM operate essential businesses with subscription-based models that generate recurring cash flows from monthly plans and enterprise contracts. Their consistent dividend payouts make them attractive to incomeseeking investors in volatile markets. Gamuda remains firmly on our top pick list, supported by its robust RM44 billion order book and RM8 billion in unbilled property sales. Its proven expertise and strong execution track record ensure continued inflows of large-scale, complex contracts, reinforcing its position as a leading infrastructure player. Sime Darby Property is equally well positioned for sustained growth, underpinned by RM3.9 billion in unbilled sales, steady recurring income from its portfolio of malls and industrial assets, and the successful delivery of hyperscale data centres. Banks remain central under the Fundamentally Solid Blue Chips theme, given their pivotal role in the economy and semi-defensive nature. Their large-scale, diversified operations, consistent dividend payouts, and highly regulated business environment under Bank Negara provide resilience despite cyclical exposure. Importantly, banks are interlinked with most investment themes, as financing will be essential for renewable energy projects, infrastructure expansion, and property development. At the same time, the rapid expansion of the digital economy is set to drive demand for digital payments, fintech innovation, and broader financial intermediation. Our top picks remainMaybank (Buy, TP:RM13.30), CIMB (Buy, TP:RM9.27), and ABMB (Buy, TP:RM5.90), which combine scale, strong franchises, and consistent shareholder returns, positioning them well to capture opportunities across both cyclical recovery and structural growth themes. Earlier, we highlightedIHH (Buy, TP:RM10.30)under the Robust Consumption and Tourism theme for 2026. While a prolonged war may weigh on medical tourism, it does not diminish the essential nature of healthcare services across IHHs diversified footprint in Malaysia, Singapore, India, Türkiye, and Europe. We continue to favour the stock for its defensive qualities and attractive valuation, trading at 13.5x EV/EBITDA for CY26 and 12.5x for CY27. This compares favourably against the upcoming Sunway Healthcare IPO, which is expected to list at 25.7x EV/EBITDA for CY26 and 21x for CY27, and could serve as a rerating catalyst for the private hospital segment. Source:TA Research- 9 Mar 2026
